Start Here · One-page Executive Summary v3.34

Production reference and market map for agentic AI systems

This document is a practical field guide for designing, choosing, operating, and governing agentic AI systems. Use it to move from product intent to architecture choices, from architecture choices to implementation stacks, and from implementation stacks to production controls.

Who should read what
ExecutivesRead this summary, the brief version, production rules, and risk checklist.
Product leadsStart with product patterns, use-case decisions, and anti-patterns.
EngineersUse the stack map, revised architecture, default stacks, state, tools, and deployment sections.
ML / DS teamsFocus on retrieval, evals, data quality, model gateways, and the ML/DS bridge.
Platform / securityPrioritize observability, identity, policy gates, audit trails, rollback, and freshness controls.
Top 10 production rules
  1. Do not add agents where deterministic workflows are enough.
  2. Separate planning, execution, memory, retrieval, and evaluation.
  3. Treat tools as privileged interfaces with permissions and audit logs.
  4. Log decisions, tool calls, inputs, outputs, and failure paths.
  5. Version prompts, schemas, tools, embeddings, evals, and policies.
  6. Keep human approval on irreversible or high-risk actions.
  7. Make retrieval freshness and source provenance first-class controls.
  8. Design rollback, timeout, and graceful-degradation paths before launch.
  9. Measure task success and operational risk, not only model quality.
  10. Prefer small, inspectable loops over broad opaque autonomy.
Compressed stakeholder brief

What to share when readers only have five minutes

Default recommendationStart with the smallest reliable workflow, add retrieval and tools only where they improve task success, and add autonomy last.
Core riskAgentic systems fail through poor tool boundaries, stale context, weak observability, and missing approval gates.
Production minimumEvery serious deployment needs evals, audit logs, rollback paths, identity controls, and freshness discipline.
Decision lensChoose architecture by task risk, latency tolerance, human review needs, data freshness, and integration complexity.
Fastest way to use this reference
30 minutesRead the Executive Summary, Start Here stacks, and Use-Case Decision Tree.
90 minutesAdd the System Map, Data Substrate, Default Stack Recipes, and Production Launch Gates.
Deep reviewUse the Market Map only after choosing the layer you need to evaluate.
Maintenance ruleTreat vendor/model details as volatile; review source links and freshness badges before decisions.
// Choose your path

How to use this page

Use this as a fast navigation layer when the full reference is more than you need. Each path points to the few sections that matter most for that build or review motion.

Building a RAG appData substrate → embedding pipeline → evals → lineage.
Building a coding agentagent patterns → tool safety → testing pyramid → failure playbook.
Building an internal workflow agentpattern selection → identity → HITL → RACI.
Doing security reviewthreat model → injection defense → privacy governance → risk register.
Choosing toolsmarket map → build-vs-buy → vendor risk → default stacks.
Productionizing an agentlaunch gates → latency → SLOs → incident response.
// Modular reading modes

Use the reference as four smaller documents

When the full document is too much, split the reading path by audience and decision type. This keeps durable architecture guidance separate from fast-changing market choices.

01Executive BriefUse the executive summary, top production rules, default stacks, build-vs-buy matrix, and launch readiness gates to align stakeholders before tool selection.
02Architecture GuideUse the revised agent stack, system map, data substrate, control-loop taxonomy, and canonical production architecture to design the system shape.
03Tool / Market MapUse the project catalog, comparisons, recommendation strength, and vendor-risk checklist only after the required layer and capability are clear.
04Production PlaybookUse evals, security, identity, tool safety, memory governance, cost controls, SLOs, incident templates, and ownership/RACI to operate the system.
// Document Guide

How to use this reference

This v3.34 update adds depth across graph databases and GraphRAG: decision table, construction pipeline, chunking strategies, hybrid retrieval patterns, schema design guidance, incremental update operations, cost model, Neptune Analytics clarification, Apache AGE caveats, and GraphRAG anti-patterns. Use it in four modes: first understand the durable architecture, then choose a tool layer from the market map, then validate the managed-platform path if relevant, and finally operate the stack through production gates, SLOs, security controls, incident playbooks, and freshness reviews.

New to the stack?Start with the system overview, maps, primitives, and complexity spectrum.Choosing tools?Use the project catalog and decision guides.Using AWS?Review the AgentCore Platform Track for the managed route.Going to production?Use launch gates, SLOs, cost architecture, risk, and incident playbooks.
Start Here · Executive Summary · v3.34

What to use, what to avoid, and what changed

This reference is split into two mental modes: Production Reference for durable system design, and Market Map for fast-changing tools, vendors, protocols, and model-provider choices. Start with the default architecture path, then consult the market map only when a concrete layer needs a product decision.

Durable referenceVolatile market mapProduction-first

Use by default

  • RAG or workflow automation before autonomous agents.
  • A graph/runtime layer when tasks require state, branching, retries, or long-running execution.
  • Hybrid retrieval, explicit state, observability, evals, security boundaries, and launch gates.
  • A model gateway when routing, cost control, fallback, logging, or provider abstraction matters.

Avoid by default

  • Agents without traces, evals, cost budgets, rollback paths, or human approval for high-impact actions.
  • Long-context prompting as a substitute for retrieval, permissions, and provenance.
  • Vendor lock-in before requirements stabilize.
  • Tool catalogs that do not separate default, strong, niche, watch, and deprecated-risk options.

Changed in v3.34

  • Added GraphRAG vs RAG decision table across 13 dimensions.
  • Added 6-stage graph knowledge construction pipeline with failure modes and tooling.
  • Added GraphRAG chunking guidance: entity boundary preservation, dual pipeline strategy.
  • Added three named hybrid retrieval patterns: entity-anchored, community summarization, path-constrained.
  • Added graph schema design guidance and GraphRAG cost model section.
  • Added incremental graph update operations table, Neptune Analytics clarification, Apache AGE maturity caveats, and 4 GraphRAG-specific anti-pattern cards.
Maintenance

Freshness and review policy

The stable architecture sections should age slowly; model/provider/tool claims should be treated as time-sensitive market information.

Last reviewed: May 31, 2026Architecture: semiannual reviewMarket map: monthly/quarterly review
Freshness policyReview production architecture, security, evals, and data governance semiannually. Review tool/vendor categories quarterly. Review model-provider, protocol, gateway, and managed-platform claims monthly when used for procurement or production decisions.
Most likely to go stale
  • Model rankings, context windows, pricing, and latency claims
  • Vendor feature maturity and managed-platform coverage
  • Protocol adoption status and ecosystem support
  • Security/compliance positioning and certifications
Most durable
  • Control-loop and orchestration concepts
  • State vs memory distinctions
  • Retrieval, eval, observability, and launch-gate discipline
  • Anti-patterns and production-readiness criteria
Freshness badge legend
Stable durable concept or architecture pattern · Current recently reviewed tool or claim · Watch fast-moving or emerging area · Audit verify before production reliance.
Glossary · operational definitions

How this document uses key terms

These definitions are intentionally operational and alphabetized for lookup. They clarify terms that are often used inconsistently across agent, AI app, infrastructure, graph, retrieval, and ML tooling discussions.

AgentA system that can choose actions over multiple steps using tools, state, memory, feedback, policies, and termination criteria.
Apache AGEA PostgreSQL extension that adds graph database capabilities and Cypher-style graph querying inside a Postgres-centered stack.
CypherA declarative graph query language used heavily with Neo4j to match nodes, relationships, paths, and graph patterns.
DatabricksEnterprise lakehouse and AI platform used as a governed data, ML lifecycle, vector search, and evaluation backbone for agents; complements agent frameworks rather than replacing them.
Entity ResolutionThe process of identifying when different names, aliases, records, or mentions refer to the same real-world entity.
EvaluationMeasurement of quality, reliability, safety, groundedness, cost, latency, and task success before and after release.
GatewayA control layer between apps and model/tool providers for routing, policy, logging, fallback, cost control, and abstraction.
Graph DatabaseA database optimized for storing and querying entities and their relationships, especially when paths, neighborhoods, lineage, or multi-hop connections matter.
Graph TraversalThe act of moving across relationships in a graph to find connected entities, paths, dependencies, permissions, or evidence chains.
GraphRAGA retrieval-augmented generation pattern that retrieves graph entities, relationships, paths, neighborhoods, communities, and provenance instead of only text chunks.
HarnessThe developer-facing wrapper around prompts, tools, tests, state, policies, and runtime behavior.
Knowledge GraphA structured graph of entities, relationships, facts, claims, metadata, and provenance used to support reasoning, retrieval, lineage, and explanation.
MemoryInformation preserved across turns or sessions for personalization, continuity, preferences, learned user context, project history, or long-running task context.
Multi-hop RetrievalRetrieval that requires more than one relationship or reasoning step, such as finding how two entities connect through intermediate documents, systems, people, or events.
Neo4jA graph-native property graph database commonly used for knowledge graphs, GraphRAG, recommendations, entity relationship traversal, fraud/compliance graphs, and dependency analysis.
Node / Relationship / PropertyCore property-graph concepts: nodes represent entities, relationships represent directed connections, and properties store attributes on either nodes or relationships.
OrchestrationThe logic that decides what happens next: routing, branching, retries, delegation, tool use, approvals, and stopping.
Property GraphA graph model where entities are represented as nodes, connections as relationships, and both can carry key-value properties.
ProtocolA shared interface standard that lets models, tools, agents, apps, or UIs interoperate.
ProvenanceTraceable evidence about where data, claims, relationships, retrieved context, tool results, or generated outputs came from.
RuntimeThe execution environment that manages steps, tool calls, retries, persistence, streaming, scheduling, and checkpointing.
SnowflakeEnterprise data cloud and AI platform used for governed SQL analytics, Cortex-powered text-to-SQL/search, internal data apps, and business-facing agent data access; complements orchestration frameworks rather than replacing them.
StateThe explicit machine-readable representation of workflow progress, task variables, checkpoints, and execution context.
Vector IndexA searchable index over embedding vectors used to find semantically similar chunks, entities, nodes, documents, or relationships.
// Start Here · Default Stack Recipes

Five recommended starting stacks

Use these as first-pass defaults before browsing the full catalog. Each recipe favors the smallest stack that can reach production safely; add autonomy, memory, multi-agent orchestration, or custom protocols only after the launch gates are measurable.

Prototype chatbotFast internal demoStreamlit or Next.js, one hosted frontier model, basic prompt templates, lightweight tracing, and a small golden set.
  • Skip memory unless users return across sessions.
  • Keep tools read-only until evals exist.
Enterprise RAGKnowledge assistantNext.js or Streamlit, ACL-aware retrieval, citation-first responses, document ingestion pipeline, eval CI, and trace capture.
  • Prioritize corpus quality and freshness.
  • Measure citation accuracy before adding actions.
Tool-using agentBounded internal operatorLangGraph or a durable workflow runtime, typed tool schemas, approval gates, state checkpointing, and rollback-safe actions.
  • Start with narrow tasks and explicit stop conditions.
  • Use HITL for irreversible operations.
Regulated systemHigh-assurance assistantPrivate retrieval, tenant-scoped data access, PII controls, policy-as-code, immutable audit logs, red-team evals, and formal launch gates.
  • Default to least privilege.
  • Log decisions, sources, and user approvals.
AWS-native pathManaged platform routeBedrock-hosted models, managed runtime/memory where appropriate, identity integration, observability, guardrails, and AWS-native deployment controls.
  • Choose this when platform governance matters more than portability.
  • Document vendor assumptions and exit constraints.
// Source & Maintenance Discipline

Separate durable architecture from volatile market claims

The document mixes stable design patterns with fast-moving model, vendor, and protocol details. Treat architecture principles as durable, but require date-stamped source checks for model names, product capabilities, pricing, launch status, and managed-service features.

Claim TypeExamplesReview CadenceEvidence Required
Stable patternLeast privilege, eval gates, typed tools, HITL, durable checkpointsAnnualArchitecture review notes or internal standards
Current-market claimModel families, tool availability, managed platform featuresMonthlyOfficial docs, release notes, or dated vendor pages
Protocol detailMCP, A2A, AG-UI, A2UI, MCP-UI semantics and adoptionQuarterlySpecification link, version, and date checked
Risk / compliance claimData handling, auditability, region support, policy controlsBefore launch and after material changesSecurity review, legal review, or compliance evidence
Maintenance rule: every volatile claim should carry a source URL, owner, and “last checked” date in the canonical version of this reference. If a claim is not source-linked, downgrade it from recommendation to research note.
// Anti-Patterns

When not to add agentic complexity

Use this section as a brake. Most production failures come from adding autonomy before the system has observability, evaluation, access control, rollback, and clear user value.

Do not use agents when a workflow is deterministic.Prefer: rules, queues, cron jobs, SQL, or standard workflow orchestration when the task does not require reasoning or ambiguity handling.
Do not add memory before you know what should persist.Prefer: explicit user profiles, scoped preferences, and auditable state before implicit long-term memory.
Do not add multi-agent orchestration to fix unclear requirements.Prefer: one reliable agent with a narrow objective, clear tool contracts, and measurable success criteria.
Do not use RAG as a substitute for content governance.Prefer: curated corpora, ACL-aware indexing, deduplication, freshness metadata, and citation evaluation.
Do not give write tools to unmeasured systems.Prefer: read-only tools, sandbox writes, dry runs, approvals, and rollback plans until evals and monitoring pass.
Do not optimize model choice before measuring task quality.Prefer: a small golden dataset, task-specific evals, latency/cost traces, and side-by-side model regression tests.
🔬

For ML & Data Science Practitioners

How your existing skills map to the agentic AI stack — and what's genuinely new

If you know machine learning or data science, you already understand more of this stack than you think. Agentic AI is not a replacement for ML — it's inference-time compute applied to goals rather than tokens. This section maps concepts you know to concepts in this document, so you can navigate the rest of it with confidence.

// Concept mapping: what you know → what this document calls it
Memory
Context window ≈ Working memory / RAM
An LLM's context window is finite (~200k tokens) — everything the model can "see" right now. When it fills, you need external memory (Mem0) or retrieval (RAG). This is why vector stores exist: they extend context beyond what fits in the window.
Retrieval
RAG ≈ Retrieval-augmented inference
RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) is retrieval-augmented inference: instead of embedding all knowledge in model weights, you retrieve relevant documents at inference time and inject them into context. You already know embedding models and nearest-neighbor search — those are the DS-familiar pieces of the RAG stack.
Control loops
ReAct / MCTS / Beam Search ≈ Inference-time search
Control loops are search algorithms run at inference time over goals rather than tokens. ReAct is a greedy search loop. Tree-of-Thought branches and prunes. MCTS (Monte Carlo Tree Search) — which you may know from AlphaGo — is the same algorithm applied to reasoning traces rather than game boards.
Evaluation
Agent eval ≈ Online experiment design
Agent evaluation is closer to online A/B testing of decision policies than offline model benchmarking. Instead of comparing predictions vs. ground truth, you compare trajectories — did the agent take the right sequence of actions? Golden datasets here are (input, expected trajectory) pairs rather than (input, label) pairs.
Observability
MLflow / experiment tracking ≈ Langfuse / LangSmith traces
You know MLflow for experiment tracking. Langfuse and LangSmith are its agentic equivalent — they trace not just model calls but tool invocations, context assembly, retrieval results, and token costs across every step of a run. Same concept, more dimensions.
Features vs. context
Feature store ≈ Context + retrieval layer
In traditional ML, a feature store serves engineered features to a model at serving time. In agentic AI, the retrieval layer plus in-context injection serves the same function — assembling the right facts into context before inference. The "features" are now retrieved passages, structured records, and tool outputs rather than numerical vectors.
// Traditional ML vs. agentic AI — a quick comparison
TL;DR before this long table
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
DimensionTraditional ML / DSAgentic AI
Core loopTrain → deploy → predict (batch or online)Prompt → think → act → observe → repeat until done
InferenceSingle forward pass, fixed outputMulti-step, tool-augmented, adaptive
KnowledgeEncoded in model weights at training timeRetrieved from external stores at inference time (RAG)
EvaluationOffline metrics: accuracy, F1, AUC on held-out setTrajectory quality, tool-call accuracy, golden datasets, human review
DeploymentModel artifact + serving infra + feature pipelineModel + harness + tools + memory + retrieval + observability
DriftData drift, concept drift, model degradationPrompt drift, retrieval staleness, tool contract changes, model version changes
Your roleBuild models, tune hyperparameters, feature engineerDesign agents, curate retrieval corpora, build eval sets, tune context
✓ Skills that transfer directly
  • Embedding models (the RAG retrieval foundation)
  • Re-rankers and cross-encoders (retrieval quality)
  • Chunking strategies (context quality for RAG)
  • Precision/recall thinking (retrieval evaluation)
  • Experiment design and A/B testing (agent eval)
  • Feature store patterns (context assembly design)
  • Monitoring and drift detection (observability)
  • Fine-tuning domain models (task-specific performance)
→ New concepts to learn
  • Context window management (working memory limits)
  • Tool schemas and MCP (how agents call external APIs)
  • Control loops (ReAct, Plan–Execute–Reflect)
  • Prompt engineering and system prompts
  • Trajectory evaluation (did the agent choose the right steps?)
  • HITL patterns (human-in-the-loop approval gates)
  • Guardrails and policy enforcement
  • Agent harnesses (Claude Code, LangGraph runtimes)
The key insight: In traditional ML, your job is to build a model that encodes knowledge at training time and produces predictions at serving time. In agentic AI, your job is to build a system that assembles knowledge at inference time, decides what actions to take, and improves from feedback over time. Your embedding models, retrieval skills, and evaluation instincts are load-bearing in the agentic stack — they're not obsolete, they're promoted.
Part I

System Overview

Start here to understand the stack, product patterns, primitives, and system maps before browsing individual tools.
Section TL;DR
  • The stack has 16 layers; every layer exists to feed, constrain, observe, or govern the model layer.
  • A chatbot is an agent with fewer layers active - same stack, lower autonomy.
  • Use the complexity spectrum to pick which layers you need before adding tooling.
  • Control loops (ReAct, Plan-Execute-Reflect, HITL) are execution policies, not architecture layers.
  • Data substrate quality and permissions matter more than model choice.

// Revised Agent Stack · Architecture Boundaries

The production agent stack: layers, inner loop, and cross-cutting planes

Use this as the canonical mental model for placing agent components. The vertical stack describes implementation boundaries; the inner loop describes the agent's behavior; the side planes describe controls that instrument, evaluate, secure, and govern more than one layer at a time.

Layer placement cheat sheet
TL;DR before this long table
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
QuestionAnswer / Layer
Who owns the user workflow, permissions, approvals, and audit posture?Product / Control Plane
What starts the run and manages the session or workspace?Agent Harness
What defines agents, prompts, model calls, tools, and structured outputs?Agent Framework
What manages state, retries, checkpoints, graph transitions, and interrupts?Agent Runtime
What centralizes model access, routing, fallback, spend, budgets, and provider abstraction?Model Gateway / AI Gateway — LiteLLM, Portkey, OpenRouter, Kong AI Gateway, Cloudflare AI Gateway
What exposes external capabilities to the agent?Context + Capability Interface — MCP, function tools, OpenAPI tools, resources, prompts
Where do side effects actually happen?Execution Substrate — sandbox, browser, database, filesystem, queue, SaaS API
What watches, scores, secures, and governs the whole path?Cross-cutting planes — observability, evals, security, governance, privacy, cost, policy
Vertical implementation stack
What owns the product surface, agent session, reusable abstractions, durable execution, tool boundary, and actual side effects.
Layer 0
CONTROL
Product / Control Plane
Business workflow, UX, auth, permissions, approvals, audit, quotas, launch gates, admin dashboards, workflow catalog.
Layer 1
HARNESS
Agent Harness
Claude Code, Cursor, Deep Agents, CLI scripts, Slack bots, scheduled jobs, service entrypoints, workspace/session management.
Layer 2
FRAMEWORK
Agent Framework
LangChain, OpenAI Agents SDK, Anthropic SDKs, , Semantic Kernel: prompts, tools, agents, model calls, structured outputs.
Layer 3
RUNTIME
Agent Runtime
LangGraph, Temporal, Prefect, Inngest, durable state machines, checkpointing, fan-out/fan-in, retries, interrupts, HITL.
Layer 3.5
MODEL GATEWAY
Model Gateway / AI Gateway
LiteLLM, Portkey, OpenRouter, Kong AI Gateway, Cloudflare AI Gateway: provider abstraction, model routing, retries, fallbacks, virtual keys, budgets, spend tracking, caching, and policy hooks.
Layer 4
INTERFACE
Context + Capability Interface
MCP, function tools, OpenAPI tools, resources, prompts, retrieval connectors, capability discovery, schemas, auth boundary.
Layer 5
EXECUTION
Execution Substrate / Sandboxes / External Systems
Docker, Kubernetes, microVMs, browser sandboxes, Python kernels, databases, filesystems, SaaS APIs, queues, storage.
Cross-cutting planes Observability, evaluation, security, governance, privacy, cost, policy, and incident response do not live in one layer. They instrument or constrain the harness, framework, runtime, tools, sandbox, and product workflow.
Inner behavioral loop
Think → Act → Observe
ReAct, plan-and-execute, reflection, evaluator-optimizer, and tree search run inside the framework/runtime. They call outward through tool interfaces and receive observations from the execution substrate.
Boundary rule LiteLLM-style gateways standardize and govern model access; MCP standardizes tool/resource access. MCP exposes a capability; the sandbox, database, browser, filesystem, or SaaS service performs the side effect. LangSmith observes traces across layers; it is not itself the runtime.
Model path vs. tool path
Model path
Agent Framework / Runtime → LiteLLM or AI Gateway → OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Bedrock, Azure OpenAI, Groq, Together, vLLM, Ollama, or another inference backend.
Tool path
Agent Framework / Runtime → MCP or tool interface → sandbox, database, browser, filesystem, SaaS API, queue, or storage system.
Read the stack downward, then read controls sideways: product/control plane decides what may run; the harness starts the agent session; the framework provides reusable agent abstractions; the runtime manages state over time; the capability interface exposes tools/resources/prompts; the execution substrate safely performs side effects. Observability, evals, security, and governance wrap the whole path.

Capability Map

// Agentic AI capability map — click any layer to explore
Click any layer to see a description.

Agentic AI System Map

🖥️UI / APP LAYERThe human-facing interface — everything below this is invisible to the end user
Streamlit
Snowflake · Python-first UI
Rapid Python-native UI for data and agent apps · no frontend experience needed · ideal for internal dashboards, agent control panels, quick prototypes · native streaming + chat support
Next.js + React
Vercel · production web stack
Standard for production agent UIs · server components · streaming via Vercel AI SDK · API routes as agent endpoints · used by most serious production chat and copilot interfaces
CopilotKit
React-first embedded copilot framework
Application-layer framework for embedding AI copilots directly into product UIs · sidebars, in-app chat, state-aware actions, approvals, and agentic workflows · ideal when the assistant should live inside an existing React app
UI patterns:Chat UI — conversational, streaming thread  ·  Dashboard UI — trigger runs, monitor progress, inspect traces  ·  Embedded Copilot — assistant inside an existing product surface (CopilotKit)  ·  IDE — dev environment with embedded harness (Cursor · Copilot · Codex)  ·  Headless / API — no UI, triggered by schedule or webhook (Airflow in Orchestrator layer)
Self-hostable UI sub-layer — pre-built interfaces you can run yourself
🌐 OpenWebUI & OpenTerminal  ready-made chat and terminal interfaces · no custom frontend required
OpenWebUI· self-hostable chat UI
Polished browser-based chat interface for LLMs and agents · supports multiple backends · useful when you want a team-facing UI without building one from scratch
OpenTerminal· terminal-native UI
CLI-style interface for developer-facing agent access · good when the natural workflow is terminal-first instead of browser-first
Treat these as UI products, not runtimes: they sit above the agent pipeline and expose a usable interface to humans.
IDE / Dev Environment sub-layer — UI and harness in one
💻 IDE Harnesses & Cloud Agents  AI-native dev environments · straddle UI and Harness layers
Cursor· most adopted
VS Code fork · multi-model · Composer agent mode · .cursorrules config · interactive AI coding · local
GitHub Copilot· enterprise
Microsoft / OpenAI · VS Code extension + JetBrains · Copilot Workspace · enterprise default on GitHub · local
Codex· cloud agent
OpenAI · fully managed cloud coding agent · sandboxed container · async · parallel tasks · opens PRs · no local interface
Claude Code (CLI · local · headless) · Cursor (GUI · local · interactive) · Copilot (extension · local · enterprise) · Codex (cloud · async · parallel — closest to Claude Managed Agents)
API / Gateway sub-layer — sits between UI and the agent pipeline
🔗 Flask & FastAPI  Python API gateway frameworks · HTTP boundary between agent pipeline and consumers
Flask· 2010 · sync-first
Battle-tested Python micro framework · HTTP routing + request handling · enormous installed base · maximum simplicity · best for straightforward integrations and existing codebases
FastAPI· 2018 · recommended default
Native async/await — fits LangGraph + Claude Agent SDK natively · auto OpenAPI docs at /docs · schema validation · first-class StreamingResponse for token-by-token output · use for all new agent API work
Use cases: POST /run agent endpoint · webhook receiver · SSE streaming to browser · subagent microservice · auth middleware
surfaced via ▼
📡GENERATIVE UI / UI PROTOCOLS — AGENT ↔ UI COMMUNICATIONThe last mile · AG-UI streams events · A2UI/MCP-UI generate surfaces · Open-JSON-UI schemas · compose with MCP and A2A
AG-UI Protocol
Anthropic / community · event streaming · agent↔UI
Streams tool calls · state updates · intermediate steps · final output to the frontend in real time · the MCP equivalent for agent↔UI · analogy: HTTP (the transport layer)
A2UI Protocol
Google · Apache 2.0 · v0.8 stable · declarative UI generation
Agents generate declarative component trees the client renders natively · Angular · Flutter · React · Lit · secure by design (pre-approved catalog) · uses A2A as transport · MCP-native · analogy: HTML (the content layer)
MCP-UI
MCP ecosystem · UI surfaces through MCP contexts
Lets MCP tools/resources return trusted interactive surfaces · forms · previews · charts · task panes · bridges MCP Protocol and Generative UI
Open-JSON-UI
Open declarative UI pattern · JSON schema
Validated JSON contract for layouts · components · actions · bindings · safe portable rendering without arbitrary code execution
Protocol map:   MCP — agent ↔ tool  ·  A2A — agent ↔ agent  ·  AG-UI / A2UI / MCP-UI / Open-JSON-UI — agent ↔ UI (last mile)  ·  A2UI uses A2A transport · MCP-UI uses MCP contexts · Open-JSON-UI gives renderer-safe schemas
triggers / displays output of ▼
🎯MULTI-AGENT ORCHESTRATORCoordinates specialist subagents · fan-out · synthesis · above the harness layer
Outer Orchestration — DAG / Workflow
Airflow
Apache · enterprise DAG orchestration · your pipeline
Enterprise standard · complex DAG dependencies · battle-tested at scale · SLA tracking · rich ecosystem · triggers LangGraph / Agent SDK runs on cron or event
Agent Teams — Supervisor / Worker
Claude Agent Teams
Anthropic · Claude-native · supervisor / worker
Supervisor routes tasks to role-based specialist agents · built on Agent SDK · MCP tools + hooks + memory inherited · natural escalation from single-agent pipeline · Claude-only
LangGraph Multi-Agent Supervisor
LangChain · LangGraph · model-agnostic · supervisor / worker
LLM supervisor node decides which worker subgraph to invoke next · each worker is a full subgraph with own tools + system prompt · supervisor reads TypedDict state · signals FINISH when done · checkpointable · model-agnostic
Subagent Spawning — Dynamic Fan-out
Claude Subagent Spawning
Anthropic · Agent SDK · Task() · dynamic at runtime
Parent agent spawns N child agents at runtime via Task() · children run concurrently · parent awaits all results then synthesises · number of children decided at runtime not at definition time · Claude-only
LangGraph Send() API
LangChain · fan-out primitive · model-agnostic · dynamic
Dispatches N subgraph instances in parallel at runtime from Send() list · each writes to shared TypedDict state · fan-out count decided at runtime · your DuckDB + OpenSearch pipeline pattern · model-agnostic
Patterns:DAG — Airflow (outer)  ·  Supervisor → workers — Agent Teams · LangGraph Supervisor (pre-defined roles)  ·  Fan-out / fan-in — Task() · Send() (dynamic N at runtime)  ·  Peer-to-peer — A2A Protocol
delegates to ▼
↔️A2A — AGENT-TO-AGENT PROTOCOLHorizontal · cross-ecosystem agent communication · Google · open standard
What it solves: cross-vendor agent delegation — Claude Code subagents talking to LangGraph agents, Google Vertex AI agents, or third-party specialist agents without bespoke integrations  ·  Analogy: TCP/IP — peer-to-peer, horizontal, ecosystem-agnostic  ·  Relevant when: enterprise multi-team interop · Vertex AI ↔ Claude handoffs · open agent marketplace
enforced within ▼
🛡️SECURITY, GOVERNANCE & TRUSTPolicy envelope around the harness · five concerns · fires in sequence
① Entitlements
Is this user allowed to ask this?
→
② RBAC
Can this role invoke this agent / tool?
→
③ Policies
Is this action within agent's allowed behaviour?
→ agent executes →
④ Guardrails
Is this output safe to return?
→
⑤ Filtering
Is this data the user can see?
Guardrails AI
Input/output content safety · validators-as-code
Schema enforcement · PII detection · off-topic blocking · hallucination detection · prompt injection prevention · runs at hook points (PreToolUse · PostToolUse · Stop)
Bedrock Guardrails
AWS · managed · zero infrastructure · your stack
Topic denial · content filters · PII detection + redaction · grounding checks (hallucination) · word blocklists · zero infra · attaches to any Claude on Bedrock call · IAM-native · complements Guardrails AI for custom validation
OPA — Open Policy Agent
CNCF · policy-as-code · Rego · RBAC / ABAC / entitlements
Harness asks OPA "can this role invoke this MCP tool on this resource?" → OPA returns allow/deny with reason · policies live in repo not application code · already standard in K8s · API gateways · now applied to agent stacks

AgentCore Identity
AWS · AgentCore · managed alternative for AWS-native stacks
Agent identity, access and authentication · compatible with Cognito · Okta · Azure Entra ID · Auth0 · any IdP · no user migration · replaces bespoke auth wiring for AWS-native stacks
AgentCore Policy
AWS · AgentCore · managed alternative for AWS-native stacks
Natural language → Cedar policies automatically · intercepts every tool call via AgentCore Gateway before execution · deterministic control · no reasoning loop slowdown · AWS-native alternative to OPA
wraps ▼

☁️ AWS-Managed Tool Sandboxes — AgentCore Code Interpreter + Browser
AgentCore Code Interpreter
AWS · AgentCore · managed alternative for AWS-native stacks
Isolated managed sandbox for agent code execution · Python · JavaScript · TypeScript · zero infrastructure · no container setup · AWS-native alternative to E2B / Modal
AgentCore Browser
AWS · AgentCore · managed alternative for AWS-native stacks
Managed cloud browser runtime · agents navigate sites, fill forms, extract data · Playwright + BrowserUse compatible · zero infrastructure · AWS-native alternative to self-hosted browser automation
Same layer, different roles: Execution-first harnesses — Claude Code · Deep Agents  ·  Runtimes — Claude Agent SDK · LangGraph
🌿 Git Worktrees — harness-level parallelism primitive
Not infrastructure — a harness primitive. Spin up N worktrees → run N Claude Code instances in parallel, each isolated in its own working directory, all sharing .git history. Near-zero overhead vs Docker containers.  ·  git worktree add ../feature-a · git worktree add ../feature-b  ·  vs Docker: worktrees isolate filesystem only · Docker isolates full environment
Agentic primitives — the internal anatomy of Claude Code & Agent SDK
Internal Primitives  the vocabulary you use to program agent behavior
🤖 Subagents
Delegate tasks to fresh Claude instances with own context windows · fan-out primitive · reaches into Orchestrator layer
⚡ Hooks
Event listeners on agent lifecycle · pre-tool · post-tool · on-error · injection point for Langfuse tracing · reaches into Observability layer
📦 Skills
Reusable capability bundles · prompt + tools + instructions packaged · can bundle RAG retrieval · reaches into Knowledge layer
/ Slash Commands
Custom invocable actions in .claude/commands/ · CLI macros · invocation primitive · user-defined agent workflows

calls tools via ▼
↕️CONTEXT + CAPABILITY INTERFACE — MCPVertical capability boundary · tools/resources/prompts · schemas · auth boundary
What it solves: standardized capability exposure — tools, resources, prompts, schemas, and auth boundary  ·  Analogy: USB-C — vertical, device-to-peripheral, plug-and-play  ·  Complements A2A: MCP is vertical (agent→tool) · A2A is horizontal (agent→agent)
☁️ AWS-Managed MCP Server Factory — AgentCore Gateway
AgentCore Gateway
AWS · AgentCore · managed alternative for AWS-native stacks
Converts any API, Lambda function, or existing service into an MCP-compatible tool in a few lines of code · connects to pre-existing MCP servers · built-in integrations: Salesforce · Zoom · JIRA · Slack · intercepts tool calls for Policy enforcement · AWS-native alternative to writing your own MCP server wrappers
APIs & CLIs → MCP — the raw substrate MCP wraps
API / CLI → MCP wrapping pattern
REST API → MCP: OpenSearch · GitHub · Power BI · Slack · Notion
CLI → MCP:git · docker · kubectl · aws · gh · terraform
APIs and CLIs are the same category — raw interfaces. MCP is the typed, discoverable protocol layer on top.
Three ways to call an API or CLI from an agent
1. Direct — requests.get() / subprocess.run() — raw, untyped, hard to observe
2. Wrapped as MCP server — typed · schema-validated · observable · recommended
3. Claude Code bash/fetch tool — direct access within controlled harness
reads from / writes to ▼
🧠MEMORY LAYERWhat the agent remembers across sessions
Mem0
Mem0 AI · embeddable middleware
Lightweight · extracts + stores memories via API · drops into any existing harness · low overhead · self-hostable · best for adding memory to existing pipelines
// ML/DS insight · continual learning & catastrophic forgettingCross-session memory is continual learning without weight updates — and faces the same core problem: storing new information without overwriting or contradicting prior facts. This is the catastrophic forgetting problem from continual learning research. When a user's preference changes ("I used to prefer formal tone, now I want casual"), naive memory systems either ignore the update (forgetting failure) or overwrite the prior state (interference failure). Mem0 addresses this at the extracted-fact level — deduplicate, reconcile contradictions, merge summaries — rather than updating model weights. This sidesteps gradient-based forgetting but introduces a new problem: contradiction resolution is still hard. Design memory schemas with explicit TTLs for volatile preferences vs. stable facts, and build correction mechanisms so users can delete or amend stored memories.
☁️ AWS-Managed Alternative — AgentCore Memory
Short-term
AgentCore Memory · in-session
Turn-by-turn context within a session · agents maintain immediate context without users repeating themselves
Long-term
AgentCore Memory · cross-session
Auto-extracts user preferences, facts, and session summaries · persists across sessions · replaces Mem0 for AWS-native stacks
Episodic
AgentCore Memory · learning
Captures context + reasoning + actions + outcomes · reflection agent analyzes patterns · agents improve from past experiences
AgentCore Memory replaces custom memory infrastructure for AWS-native stacks · zero infrastructure · three managed tiers · works with LangGraph, LangChain, Strands
queries for context ▼
📄DOCUMENT PROCESSING & INGESTIONRaw unstructured content in → clean embeddable text out · runs before the agent, not during
When it runs: batch / scheduled (Airflow · S3 event) — not real-time. Documents are processed and indexed ahead of time, then retrieved at agent query time via RAG.
AWS Textract
AWS · OCR + document understanding
Beyond basic OCR · understands tables · forms · key-value pairs · checkboxes · multi-column layouts · handwriting · outputs structured JSON ready for chunking and embedding · ideal for financial reports and regulatory filings
AWS Bedrock Data Automation
AWS · managed multimodal pipeline
End-to-end multimodal ingestion: Textract + classification + extraction + enrichment in one managed service · handles PDFs · images · audio · video · outputs to S3 · less control than raw Textract · far less glue code
Pipeline:  Raw docs → Textract / Bedrock Data Automation (extract + structure) → Chunking + Embedding → OpenSearch / pgvector (index) → RAG at agent query time
indexes into ▼
🗄️KNOWLEDGE & RETRIEVALDomain knowledge fed into agent context at query time
Embeddings — the foundation. Text → vectors via embedding model (OpenAI text-embedding-3, Cohere, HuggingFace local). Everything in this layer depends on them.
Vector DBs — retrieval substrate
Vector + Search
OpenSearch ★ · pgvector
Hybrid lexical + semantic retrieval · BM25 + vector + metadata filters · best when keyword precision and vector recall both matter · OpenSearch is your primary stack; pgvector keeps retrieval inside PostgreSQL
Vector (Pure-Play)
Pinecone · Weaviate · Chroma
Dedicated embedding stores · Pinecone for managed scale · Weaviate for open-source object-aware retrieval · Chroma for local-first prototypes and lightweight RAG apps
Vector (S3-Native)
Amazon S3 Vectors
Store and query embedding vectors directly in S3 · lowest-cost AWS-native option for high-volume vector storage · fits document-heavy RAG pipelines
Graph Traversal — relationship traversal over structured data
Neo4j
Graph-native property graph + GraphRAG platform
Cypher traversal · knowledge graph storage · vector indexes over graph entities · best when paths, neighborhoods, entity relationships, and provenance are first-class retrieval units
Amazon Neptune
AWS · managed graph DB
Property Graph: Gremlin / openCypher · RDF: SPARQL · Neptune Analytics adds vector search on top of graph traversal · native GraphRAG on AWS
RAG: query → embed → vector search → retrieve chunks → generate

GraphRAG: build knowledge graph from docs → graph hops + vector search → multi-hop reasoning
iterates via ▼
🔁INNER LOOP · LOOP PATTERNS & ITERATION CONTROLBehavioral control patterns that run inside the framework/runtime
Primitive + foundational execution
Ralph Loop
while → run → stop
Mechanical keep-going substrate · terminal-first · every named loop specifies what happens inside the body before completion
ReAct
Reason → Act → Observe
Foundational inner tool-calling loop · calls tools or subagents · observes result · repeats until done
Plan–Execute–Reflect
Outer structured agent loop
Plan full approach → execute stepwise → reflect on quality/failures → revise · best for long multi-step tasks
HITL + Confidence Gates
Interrupt when uncertain or policy-gated
Agent pauses for human approval/correction or routes to verification/retry based on risk and confidence
Test-Driven Loop
Observe = test result
Write → test → observe failures → revise · strongest coding feedback loop because pass/fail is explicit
Reflexion
Attempt → lesson → retry
Stores reusable self-feedback as future experience · distinct from one-off critique
Search and quality control
Search / Tree-of-Thought
Branch → score → prune
Explores multiple reasoning/action paths instead of one linear trajectory · useful for hard reasoning and planning alternatives
Best-of-N / Evolutionary
Generate candidates → evaluate → select
Improves output through parallel diversity, ranking, mutation, and selection · useful for prompt/design optimization
Critique / Debate
Draft → challenge → revise / judge
Separates producer, critic, opponent, and judge roles · surfaces hidden assumptions and improves artifact quality
Consensus / Verifier
Compare outputs · enforce constraints
Multiple agents/models reconcile disagreements · hard checks validate schemas, tests, citations, permissions, and policies
Knowledge, capability, and coordination loops
Retrieval-Grounded Research
Query → retrieve → synthesize → gap-check
Loop continues until evidence is sufficient · core RAG/deep-research pattern
Memory + Tool Discovery
Act → remember · Need → discover tool
Updates future behavior through persistent memory · acquires missing capabilities through APIs/MCP/tool catalogs
Multi-Agent Coordination
Supervisor · Contract-Net · Blackboard · Swarm
Distributes work across specialists by routing, bidding, shared workspace, or decentralized local actions
Production Control
DAG · State Machine · Event-Driven · Monitor–Act
External orchestration, explicit transitions, event triggers, and continuous environment feedback for production agents
Default composition: DAG / Workflow → Plan–Execute–Reflect → ReAct → Verifier / HITL / Memory hooks. Add Search when the solution space is broad, Critique/Consensus when quality risk is high, Retrieval when evidence is missing, and Multi-Agent coordination when specialist parallelism is needed.
persists state via ▼
💾AGENT STATE & PERSISTENCELow-latency state, SQL checkpoints, time-series telemetry, and warehouse-backed context
Low-latency KV State Stores
Redis
Redis Ltd · in-memory · ephemeral
Fast in-memory store for agent working state · pub/sub for inter-agent messaging · shared scratchpad during fan-out · LLM response caching · rate limiting
Amazon DynamoDB
AWS · serverless KV · zero infrastructure · pay-per-request
Serverless durable KV — where Redis is ephemeral and in-memory, DynamoDB is durable and serverless · session state between runs · agent config store · rate limiting · atomic counters · pay-per-request cheaper than always-on Redis for bursty workloads · IAM-native
Relational + Analytical SQL Databases
PostgreSQL
Durable checkpoint backend
LangGraph's production checkpointer backend · stores graph state snapshots · run history · audit logs · pairs with pgvector for single-DB retrieval + persistence
Amazon Redshift
AWS · analytical SQL warehouse
Warehouse-backed database source for agents · governed enterprise facts · historical metrics · audit/report tables · OLAP queries through tools · separate from Redis/DynamoDB hot state stores
LangGraph Checkpointer
LangChain · built-in
Snapshots full graph state at each node · resume interrupted runs from last checkpoint · backends: in-memory (dev) · PostgreSQL (prod) · SQLite (local)
Time-Series DBs — telemetry, high-frequency events, and operational metrics over time
Temporal persistence: use time-series stores when the agent system needs to query behavior over time — latency curves, event bursts, cost trends, traces, simulations, market-style streams, and operational anomaly windows.
TimescaleDB
Timescale · PostgreSQL-native time-series
SQL-native time-series analytics · joins metrics with relational run metadata · continuous aggregates · retention policies · good when Postgres is already the system of record
kdb+ / KX
KX · high-performance time-series analytics
High-frequency timestamped facts · market data · streaming events · agent trace analytics · latency windows · anomaly detection · best when event volume and temporal query speed dominate
Document DB — variable-schema agent artifacts
MongoDB
MongoDB Inc. · flexible schema · Atlas vector search
Default document DB · stores variable-shape agent run outputs · generated reports · cached tool results · Atlas Vector Search adds retrieval in same store · supported as LangGraph checkpointer backend
Amazon DocumentDB
AWS · managed · MongoDB-compatible
AWS-managed with full MongoDB API compatibility · zero cluster management · integrates with IAM · Secrets Manager · CloudWatch · natural choice for AWS-native stacks
observed by ▼
📊OBSERVABILITY & EVALUATION PLANECross-cutting plane — traces model, runtime, tool, retrieval, sandbox, and product events
LLM-Native Tracing — what the model did
Langfuse
Langfuse GmbH · open source
Framework-agnostic tracing · evals · prompt versioning · cost tracking · self-hostable · best for multi-framework pipelines
LangSmith
LangChain · SaaS-only
LangGraph-native · full graph viz of node-by-node execution · evals · datasets · human annotation · LangGraph Platform default
Ops / Infrastructure — what the system did
Splunk
Enterprise log aggregation
Ingests everything: server logs · agent stdout · pipeline errors · security events · infrastructure metrics · the ops observability layer alongside LLM-native tracing
Grafana
Grafana Labs · metrics & dashboards
Metrics visualization + operational dashboards · latency · throughput · error rates · cost per run · connects to Prometheus · CloudWatch · Datadog · SLA monitoring
Experiment Tracking — what you tried and learned
MLflow
Databricks · open source MLOps
Tracks prompt experiments + eval runs · manages fine-tuned model versions · model registry · most relevant when doing model fine-tuning alongside agent development · integrates with Databricks · AWS SageMaker · Azure ML
☁️ AWS-Managed Alternative — AgentCore Observability
AgentCore Observability
AWS · AgentCore · managed alternative for AWS-native stacks
Unified trace, debug, and monitor view · step-by-step execution visualization of each agent workflow step · inspect execution paths · audit intermediate outputs · debug bottlenecks and failures · OTEL-compatible · integrates with any monitoring stack · results in CloudWatch · covers LLM-native tracing + ops in one managed service
Langfuse / LangSmith — what the model did  ·  Splunk / Grafana — what the system did  ·  MLflow — what you tried and learned  ·  AgentCore Observability — all three concerns in one managed CloudWatch-backed service for AWS-native stacks
validated by ▼
🧪EVALUATION & TESTINGPre-production validation · golden datasets · regression suites · trajectory evals
PromptFoo
Open source · prompt & agent output testing
YAML-defined test cases · LLM-as-judge assertions · schema validation · CI integration via GitHub Actions · blocks merge if scores drop · the test-driven agent loop's testing framework
DeepEval
Confident AI · RAG & agent eval framework
Correctness · faithfulness · hallucination detection · contextual relevance · answer relevance · strong for RAG pipeline validation · are retrieved docs faithfully represented in output?
AgentEvals
Anthropic · trajectory-level evaluation
Evaluates decision quality not just output quality · did the agent choose the right tool in the right order? · golden trajectory datasets · trajectory comparison metrics · HITL annotation workflows · complements PromptFoo (output) with sequence-level eval
Eval loop:   PromptFoo on PR → GitHub Actions gate → Langfuse tracks production scores → DeepEval validates RAG faithfulness per run → AgentEvals debugs trajectory failures  ·  Golden datasets — curated (input, expected output) pairs that catch regressions when prompts, models, or retrieval strategies change  · 
☁️ AWS-Managed Alternative — AgentCore Evaluations
AgentCore Evaluations
AWS · AgentCore · managed alternative for AWS-native stacks
13 built-in evaluators: correctness · faithfulness · safety · tool selection accuracy · edge case handling · output reliability · custom scoring · evaluates sessions, traces, and spans from LangGraph or Strands · instrumented via OpenTelemetry or OpenInference · all results in CloudWatch alongside AgentCore Observability · AWS-native alternative to PromptFoo + DeepEval
AgentCore Evaluations — if AWS-native, replaces PromptFoo + DeepEval with a managed eval service · use AgentEvals (Anthropic) alongside for trajectory-level evaluation
runs on ▼
⚙️EXECUTION SUBSTRATE / INFRASTRUCTURESandboxes · containers · orchestration · databases · SaaS systems where side effects occur
Sandbox boundary: MCP or function calling exposes the capability; the execution substrate performs the side effect under isolation, resource limits, network policy, secrets controls, and artifact cleanup.
Container Infrastructure — the execution substrate
Docker
Container runtime · foundational primitive
Packages agent pipelines into portable containers · isolates Claude Code headless runs and subagent environments · foundation everything else runs on
Kubernetes
CNCF · container orchestration
Runs thousands of containers · schedules LangGraph workers · FastAPI endpoints · Airflow schedulers · self-healing · auto-scaling · the production fleet manager
EKS
AWS · managed Kubernetes
AWS manages the K8s control plane · natural runtime for AWS-native stacks (Textract · Neptune · S3 Vectors · Bedrock · OpenSearch) · you write K8s · AWS runs it
Temporal
Temporal Technologies · durable execution
Durable execution under LangGraph · crash-safe long-running workflows · retries · timeouts · exactly-once · pipeline completes even if a node dies mid-run
Agent-Specific Runtime — managed agent infra
Amazon Bedrock AgentCore — 9 modular services
AWS · fully managed · any framework · any model · 2025
