AWS production path:DIY: Docker → EKS → LangGraph + FastAPI + Airflow containerised  ·  Managed: AgentCore — 9 services covering Runtime · Memory · Gateway · Identity · Code · Browser · Observability · Evals · Policy
CI / CD — the automated feedback loop
GitHub Actions
GitHub / Microsoft · CI/CD automation
Agent-triggered CI: agent writes code → opens PR → Actions runs tests → results feed back to agent → agent iterates · the feedback loop that makes autonomous coding agents useful in production
Agent-as-Action:claude --headless runs inside a workflow step triggered by PR comment or label — the agent is the CI · pairs with Codex to close the full autonomous development loop
powered by ▼
🧠MODEL LAYER — the layer every other layer servesProduction model-selection architecture — choose by role, not brand
Model selection principle: Treat the model layer as a routing architecture. Use different model families for frontier reasoning, open/self-hosted deployments, coding-agent workflows, fast/cheap traffic, retrieval infrastructure, and AWS-native production stacks.
Core frontier — highest-capability generalist and reasoning models
Claude 4 family
Opus · Sonnet · Haiku
Use Opus/Sonnet for complex reasoning, coding, orchestration, long-form synthesis, and high-value agent nodes; use Haiku for fast subagent traffic.
GPT-5 / GPT-5.5 family
OpenAI · frontier / mini / nano tiers
General-purpose reasoning, coding, multimodal workflows, tool use, and cost-tiered production routing when the OpenAI ecosystem is preferred.
Gemini 2.5 / 3.x
Google · Pro / Flash / Live tiers
Strong fit for long-context, multimodal, real-time conversational, Google-native, and A2A-aligned agent workflows.
Open / self-host — data sovereignty, private deployment, and cost control
Llama 4
Meta · open-weight
Self-hosted and air-gapped deployments, private inference, open model governance, and cost control at scale.
Mistral 3 family
Mistral · Large / Medium / Small
Enterprise, EU/sovereign AI, multilingual, and self-hostable or commercially hosted stacks.
DeepSeek
Reasoning / coding / cost-performance
Cost-sensitive reasoning and coding workloads; useful as an open or API-accessible alternative in multi-model routing.
Qwen
Open / multilingual / coder variants
Multilingual agents, coding subagents, open alternatives, and China/APAC-relevant model stacks.
Gemma · Granite · Nemotron
Google · IBM · NVIDIA
Smaller/open enterprise model options for local, governed, synthetic-data, or NVIDIA-stack deployments.
Kimi K2 / K2.5
Moonshot AI · MoE · long-context
Agentic coding, tool use, long-context reasoning, and cost-efficient autonomous workflows; belongs near DeepSeek, Qwen, Llama, and Mistral.
Coding / agentic — models specialized for building, debugging, and IDE workflows
Claude Sonnet / Opus
Coding + agentic reasoning
Strong default for code generation, refactoring, repository reasoning, subagent orchestration, and tool-use loops.
GPT-5
Coding · reasoning · tool use
General coding-agent and professional-work model option when OpenAI tooling, APIs, or Codex-style workflows are preferred.
Composer 2.5
Cursor · IDE-native
Cursor-native coding-agent model for sustained IDE workflows, multi-file implementation, and long-running engineering tasks.
Devstral / Codestral
Mistral · code-specialized
Software-engineering and code-generation workloads, especially in Mistral/EU/self-hosted coding stacks.
DeepSeek · Qwen Coder
Cost-efficient coding models
Useful for coding subagents, parallel implementation tasks, and cheaper code-generation lanes.
Gemini Pro / Flash
Google · coding + long context
Repository-scale context, multimodal debugging, Google-native workflows, and low-latency coding-agent paths.
Fast / cheap — high-volume routing, classification, and lightweight subagents
Claude Haiku
Fast Claude tier
Routing, filtering, extraction, classification, moderation pre-checks, and high-volume subagent work.
GPT mini / nano
OpenAI cost tiers
Cost-sensitive traffic, simple tool selection, structured extraction, and background evaluation tasks.
Gemini Flash / Flash-Lite
Google speed tiers
Low-latency responses, large-context price-performance, and real-time interactive agent experiences.
Mistral Small
Lightweight Mistral tier
Lower-cost multilingual, classification, extraction, and simple agent tasks.
Phi
Microsoft · small model family
Edge, local, narrow-task, and low-cost extraction/classification use cases.
Small open models
Gemma · Qwen small · distilled variants
Private lightweight agents, on-device assistants, and fallback lanes where frontier capability is unnecessary.
Retrieval models — embeddings, rerankers, and search quality infrastructure
Cohere Embed / Rerank
Enterprise RAG
Strong for enterprise search, reranking, multilingual retrieval, and managed RAG pipelines.
Voyage AI
Embedding + reranking
High-quality retrieval models for domain search, legal/finance-style RAG, and reranked context assembly.
Jina · BGE / E5
Open retrieval models
Self-hostable embeddings and rerankers for private, multilingual, or open-source retrieval stacks.
OpenAI embeddings
OpenAI retrieval lane
Default fit for OpenAI-native apps, semantic search, and general-purpose embedding pipelines.
Amazon Titan Embeddings
AWS-native retrieval
Bedrock-native embedding path for AWS governance, IAM, logging, and managed infrastructure alignment.
Google text embeddings
Google / Vertex AI
Retrieval option for Google-native agent and data stacks.
AWS-native — Bedrock-first model and retrieval path
Amazon Nova
AWS-native generation
Bedrock-first model path for teams standardizing on AWS governance, IAM, monitoring, and procurement.
Titan Embeddings
AWS-native retrieval
Embedding layer for AWS-native RAG with OpenSearch, S3, Bedrock, and AgentCore-aligned stacks.
Bedrock-hosted Claude / Llama / Mistral
Managed multi-model access
Enterprise model access through Bedrock controls, IAM, centralized billing, observability, and region-aware governance.
Production routing pattern: Core frontier models handle orchestration and high-value reasoning · fast/cheap models handle routing, extraction, classification, and background workers · open/self-hosted models handle sovereignty or cost constraints · coding-specialized models handle IDE/repo workflows · retrieval models power the RAG layer · AWS-native models simplify governed Bedrock deployments.

Data Substrate for Agentic Performance

Agentic AI performance is not only a model problem. The data substrate controls how quickly an agent can assemble context, how accurately it can ground decisions, how safely it can respect permissions, and how well it can learn from traces. Treat relational, vector, graph, time-series, document, and in-memory databases as complementary performance infrastructure rather than interchangeable storage options.

🧬Agent Data Loophow data improves behavior
1 · Request
The user request enters the planner with identity, tenant, session, and policy context. This is where relational permissions and session state prevent unsafe or irrelevant work from starting.
2 · Context assembly
The agent gathers structured records, semantic passages, relationship paths, recent events, artifacts, and cache hits before the model reasons. Better context assembly usually beats larger prompts.
3 · Tool execution
The runtime calls tools with bounded schemas, transaction checks, and fresh data. Relational and in-memory systems keep actions correct and fast; graph and vector systems keep actions grounded.
4 · Response
The answer is produced with citations, provenance, policy filtering, and evidence. The database layer gives the model facts, not just memories.
5 · Feedback
Traces, eval outcomes, user corrections, events, and cost/latency metrics flow back into observability, memory, and future routing decisions.
🗃️Database Type → Agent Capabilityrole map
Relational DBImproves correctness, permissions, joins, transactions, account state, workflow state, and auditability. Use it for users, tenants, ACLs, orders, approvals, tasks, and source-of-truth business records.
PostgreSQLAuroraMySQL
Vector DBImproves semantic recall, grounding, similarity search, deduplication, and retrieval-augmented generation. Use it for passages, chunks, embeddings, multimodal search, and nearest-neighbor memory.
pgvectorpgvectorscaleOpenSearchS3 Vectors
Graph DBImproves relationship reasoning, entity linking, lineage, impact analysis, recommendations, and multi-hop retrieval. Use it when the question depends on connected entities, not only similar text.
Amazon NeptuneApache AGENeo4jGraphRAG
Graph Schema Design for Agents The highest-leverage decision in a graph system is what to model before you ingest anything. A schema designed for completeness rather than retrieval produces a graph that is expensive to traverse and hard to query.
Model entities as nodes People, organizations, documents, products, systems, concepts, risks, obligations, and events. Each node type gets a label. Node properties store attributes — name, ID, timestamp, confidence, permission tags.
Model facts as relationships WORKS_AT, DEPENDS_ON, OWNED_BY, CITED_IN, APPLIES_TO, CONNECTED_TO. Relationships should be typed, directed, and carry properties: confidence, provenance chunk ID, extraction timestamp. Avoid relationships named RELATED_TO — too vague for traversal.
Keep long text out of nodes Long document text belongs in the vector layer, not on graph nodes. Node properties should be short, structured, and queryable (name, ID, category, date, permission label). The graph and vector layers are complementary — don't duplicate content between them.
Schema design rule: Start from the questions the agent needs to answer, not from the documents you have. Work backwards from "How is A connected to B?" to the relationship type that path requires. Every node type and relationship type in your schema should be traceable to a real retrieval use case.
Time-Series DBImproves temporal awareness, trend detection, anomaly detection, monitoring, forecasting, and event memory. Use it for metrics, logs, prices, sensor streams, agent latency, and drift signals.
TimescaleDBkdb+ / KXPrometheus
Document DBImproves flexible state, semi-structured records, artifacts, conversation snapshots, evolving schemas, and tool outputs. Use it when agent state is nested, variable, or changing quickly.
MongoDBDocumentDBDynamoDB
In-Memory DBImproves latency, working memory, queues, locks, rate limits, deduplication, and session continuity. Use it for short-lived context and fast coordination, not as the only durable memory.
RedisValkeyMemcached
🐘PostgreSQL Extension Stack for Agentic Systemsretrieval · telemetry · graph · scale · operations

Postgres is not only the relational layer. Through extensions it can become the semantic retrieval layer, lexical search layer, graph layer, time-series layer, scheduler, query-observability layer, distributed SQL layer, in-database ML runtime, and analytical execution surface. Every extension registers its capabilities via PostgreSQL's CREATE EXTENSION mechanism, which runs an install script that may register custom index access methods, background workers, executor hooks, and shared memory segments. Treat extensions as composable capabilities, not random plugins.

Diagram · PostgreSQL Extension Capability Map
🐘
PostgreSQL Core
MVCC · WAL · SQL planner · indexes · transactions · extensions
Retrievalpgvector pgvectorscale
pg_search pg_textsearch
Semantic nearest-neighbor search plus lexical/BM25 recall.
Time + TelemetryTimescaleDB pg_stat_statements
Agent traces, cost curves, latency history, and SQL hot spots.
Graph + KnowledgeApache AGE
Entity relationships, graph traversal, dependency maps, and GraphRAG paths.
Automation + MLpg_cron pgml
Scheduled refreshes, cleanup jobs, backfills, and selective in-database inference.
Scale + AnalyticsCitus pg_duckdb
Tenant sharding, distributed SQL, OLAP scans, and lakehouse-style analytics.
pgvector — semantic retrieval
IVFFlat · HNSW · cosine / L2 / dot operators
pgvector adds a native vector column type and distance operators (<->, <#>, <=>) to Postgres. It registers two custom index access methods via IndexAmRoutine: IVFFlat, which partitions vectors into inverted file lists for fast approximate search; and HNSW, which builds a multi-layer navigable small-world graph where each node connects to nearby vectors at multiple zoom levels — enabling fast greedy traversal at query time with high recall. HNSW is generally preferred for production RAG. Combine with standard SQL filters, joins, and transactions without any data sync overhead.
HNSW preferred for RAGSQL + vectors in one DBzero extra infra
pgvectorscale — large-scale ANN
DiskANN · streaming graph · RAM-efficient
pgvectorscale adds a DiskANN-style streaming index. Unlike HNSW, which loads the full graph into memory, DiskANN is designed for vector datasets that exceed available RAM — it streams graph neighborhoods from disk using sequential I/O patterns that keep page faults low. The result is dramatically better recall-per-second at scale for billion-scale embedding workloads. It sits on top of pgvector (not a replacement) and registers via the same index AM interface. Graduate from pgvector HNSW to pgvectorscale when your index no longer fits in memory or you need higher QPS at scale.
billion-scale vectorsdisk-resident indexpgvector complement
TimescaleDB — temporal telemetry
hypertables · chunks · LZ4/gorilla/delta-delta compression · continuous aggregates
TimescaleDB introduces hypertables — logical tables transparently partitioned into chunks by time (and optionally by space). Queries hit only relevant chunks via constraint exclusion. Older chunks are compressed using columnar codecs (LZ4 for general data, gorilla for floats, delta-delta for timestamps) — reducing storage 10–20×. Continuous aggregates are materialized views that refresh incrementally rather than recomputing from scratch. The planner hook enables chunk-level parallelism. Use for agent traces, token costs, latency curves, eval scores over time, and drift signals. Pairs with pgvector: store embedding vectors alongside their timestamps to query "most similar embeddings in the last 7 days."
Postgres-native time-seriescontinuous aggregatesretention policies
pg_search — BM25 via Tantivy
BM25 · faceting · phrase slop · fuzzy · @@@ operator
pg_search (ParadeDB) embeds a Tantivy index (a Rust-based Lucene equivalent) as a custom Postgres index AM. Unlike native tsvector which uses simple term frequency, BM25 is a probabilistic ranking function that accounts for term frequency, inverse document frequency, and document-length normalization. It supports field boosting, phrase slop, and faceted search. Query via the @@@ operator. Stored as custom index AM structures inside Postgres buffer manager. Pair with pgvector for hybrid RAG — use BM25 for exact terms (product names, error codes, legal clauses) and vector search for semantic recall, then RRF-fuse the results. Best when you want Elasticsearch-style relevance without operating a separate cluster.
BM25 probabilistic rankinghybrid RAGno separate cluster
pg_textsearch — BM25 via Postgres FTS path
BM25 · Postgres text-search infrastructure · tickers · IDs · clauses
pg_textsearch (Timescale) leans into Postgres's native text-search plumbing (tsvector/tsquery infrastructure) while layering BM25 scoring on top. Where pg_search introduces a fully separate Tantivy index AM, pg_textsearch works closer to the standard FTS execution path, making it easier to combine with existing Postgres FTS indexes and GIN indexes. Best fit when exact tokens — tickers, IDs, legal clause identifiers, and structured error strings — must survive retrieval alongside semantic vector search. Pair with pgvector for hybrid retrieval pipelines in Postgres-centered stacks.
BM25 keyword relevancePostgres FTS pathstructured token survival
pg_cron — in-database scheduler
background worker · cron.job table · SQL or CALL invocations
pg_cron runs as a background worker (registered via Postgres's BGW API) that wakes every minute, reads the cron.job catalog table, and forks a new database connection to execute any matching jobs — SQL statements or stored procedure CALLs. This eliminates external schedulers for database-internal tasks. Use it for materialized-view refreshes, embedding backfills, retention cleanup, rollups, stale-document audits, and lightweight scheduled eval batches. Not a full workflow orchestrator — keep multi-step DAGs in Airflow, Dagster, or Temporal; use pg_cron for maintenance operations that live close to the data.
background workercron syntaxmaintenance automation
pgml / PostgresML — in-database ML
pgml.train() · pgml.predict() · pgml.embed() · Rust/PyO3 bindings
pgml embeds ML inference and training inside Postgres via Rust bindings (PyO3) to scikit-learn, XGBoost, LightGBM, and Hugging Face transformers. Functions like pgml.train(), pgml.predict(), and pgml.embed() run in-process, keeping data local and avoiding round-trips to an external Python service. You can train a gradient-boosted classifier or generate sentence embeddings in a SQL query. Best fit for controlled internal workloads and data-local inference — avoid turning the primary OLTP Postgres into a high-throughput model-serving tier unless the platform is explicitly designed for that load.
in-process inferenceRust/PyO3 bindingsdata-local ML
Citus — distributed Postgres
coordinator · workers · consistent hashing · 32 shards default · co-located joins
Citus introduces a coordinator node and multiple worker nodes. Tables are distributed by a shard key using consistent hashing — 32 shards by default, placed across workers. The coordinator receives queries, rewrites them into fragment queries targeting relevant shards, and fans them out in parallel. For co-located joins (joining on the same distribution key), Citus pushes the join down to workers entirely. It also supports reference tables (replicated to all workers) and columnar storage. The extension hooks into Postgres's executor using custom plan nodes and hooks. Use when tenant_id / account_id is a natural shard key and one node can no longer handle write throughput for documents, chunks, runs, and tool events.
tenant shardingco-located joinsscale-out Postgres
AGE — graph database in Postgres
labeled property graph · Cypher · agtype · vertex/edge tables per graph
Apache AGE adds a labeled property graph model with an openCypher query interface. Internally, vertices and edges each get their own Postgres tables per graph, with JSON properties stored as agtype (a superset of JSON). When you call cypher(), the AGE parser translates Cypher to a Postgres query plan. Supports pattern matching, path traversal, and shortest-path queries, all running on standard Postgres storage and transactions. Use when retrieval depends on relationships: entity maps, lineage, dependency paths, compliance webs, and multi-hop knowledge graphs — without adding a separate graph database to the stack.
openCypher queriesagtype JSON propertiesPostgres-native graph
pg_stat_statements — query observability
ExecutorEnd hook · normalized query hash · buffer hits · WAL writes
pg_stat_statements intercepts every SQL query via the ExecutorEnd and ExecutorStart planner hooks, normalizes it (replacing literals with placeholders), and accumulates execution stats in a shared memory hash table keyed by query hash: total calls, total/min/max/mean execution time, rows returned, buffer hits/misses, and WAL writes. Planning time is captured separately. Essential for finding slow vector searches, missing ANN filters, expensive hybrid retrieval joins, and N+1 patterns generated by ORM or agent tool layers. The production default — enable it from day one.
query statsshared memory hashproduction default
pg_duckdb — OLAP inside Postgres
vectorized columnar execution · DuckDB scan nodes · Parquet/S3 · foreign tables
pg_duckdb embeds DuckDB's analytical engine inside Postgres as an execution engine. When a query touches a duckdb foreign table or uses DuckDB-specific syntax, the extension routes it to DuckDB's vectorized columnar executor (SIMD batch processing) rather than Postgres's row-based executor. DuckDB excels at aggregations, multi-column scans, and wide-table joins. Results are returned to Postgres as normal rows. You can also read Parquet files directly from S3 via DuckDB's scanner. Treat as an OLAP accelerator for heavy analytics next to operational data — not a replacement for OLTP query discipline.
OLAP accelerationParquet / S3 scanvectorized execution
How extensions hook into PostgresMost extensions use a combination of: custom index access methods (IndexAmRoutine), background worker processes (BackgroundWorkerHandle), executor/planner hooks (ExecutorRun_hook, planner_hook), custom scan providers, and shared memory segments. CREATE EXTENSION runs the install SQL script that registers these hooks in the catalog. The extension boundary is powerful, but it is still part of your database reliability and security model.
Architecture ruleStart simple: Postgres + pgvector (HNSW) + pg_stat_statements + pg_cron. Add pg_search or pg_textsearch when exact terms matter alongside semantic recall. Add TimescaleDB when traces and latency become operational data you query over time. Add AGE when relationships become first-class retrieval paths. Add Citus when one node is not enough and tenant_id is a natural shard key. Add pg_duckdb when OLAP scans over operational data get heavy. Add pgml only when in-database inference is genuinely cheaper or simpler than an external model service. Upgrade pgvector → pgvectorscale when the HNSW index no longer fits in RAM.
Diagram · PostgreSQL Extension Selection Decision Tree
Need semantic similarity?
Yes → pgvector
Index too large for RAM → pgvectorscale
Do exact terms / IDs matter?
Yes → pg_search or pg_textsearch
Use with vector search for hybrid recall.
Are relationships first-class?
Yes → Apache AGE
Use for entity graphs, lineage, dependencies, and GraphRAG.
Are traces/time ranges core?
Yes → TimescaleDB
Use for latency, cost, evaluation, drift, and event history.
Need scheduled DB-native jobs?
Yes → pg_cron
Refresh materialized views, summarize memory, clean traces, and run backfills.
Are SQL queries opaque or slow?
Always → pg_stat_statements
Baseline observability for retrieval, joins, filters, and index behavior.
Is one Postgres node not enough?
Yes → Citus
Best when tenant_id or account/workspace ID is a natural shard key.
Need OLAP / Parquet-style scans?
Yes → pg_duckdb
Use for analytical scans without moving operational data too far away.
Need in-database ML?
Maybe → pgml
Use only when local inference is simpler or cheaper than external model serving.
🔀Hybrid Retrieval Flowhow it works

For high-value agents, retrieval should be hybrid: the system should extract intent and entities, call multiple data stores in parallel, then merge, rerank, filter, and cite evidence before the model responds.

QueryUser asks a question or requests an action.
ParseExtract intent, entities, tenant, permissions, time range, and freshness needs.
RetrieveSQL lookup + vector search + graph traversal + recent events + session cache.
AssembleRerank, dedupe, policy-filter, compress, and attach provenance.
Act / AnswerUse grounded context for response, tool call, approval request, or workflow step.
Diagram · Postgres-native hybrid retrieval path
User question → query planParse intent, tenant, permissions, entities, source constraints, time range, and freshness requirements before retrieval begins.
↓
1 · Semantic Search
pgvectorpgvectorscale
Find meaning-similar chunks with vector indexes while preserving tenant, source, and freshness filters.
2 · Keyword / BM25
pg_searchpg_textsearch
Recover exact terms, tickers, IDs, clause names, error codes, and rare entities that embeddings may smooth over.
3 · Graph Expansion
Neo4jNeptuneApache AGE
Expand from retrieved entities to connected accounts, policies, instruments, dependencies, owners, or evidence paths.
↓
Candidate mergeUnion vector, BM25, SQL, and graph results into a single candidate set before ranking.
RerankUse RRF, cross-encoder score, policy score, freshness, and source trust.
Context PackDedupe, compress, attach provenance, and fit the prompt budget.
Grounded PromptPass compact evidence, citations, constraints, and allowed actions to the model.
Answer / ActGenerate cited response, tool call, workflow step, or HITL approval request.
Why this mattersVector search gives recall by meaning; BM25 protects exactness; graph traversal adds relationship reasoning. Production RAG usually needs all three, especially for financial, compliance, engineering, and operational knowledge.
🕸️Neo4j GraphRAG Flowgraph-native retrieval

Neo4j belongs in the architecture when the retrieval unit is an entity, relationship, path, neighborhood, community, permission boundary, or evidence chain. Vector search retrieves similar chunks; graph retrieval explains how facts connect.

1 · Extract entitiesPeople, organizations, accounts, documents, systems, products, risks, claims, events, owners, and obligations.
2 · Resolve identitiesMerge aliases, duplicate entities, ticker variants, account IDs, system names, and conflicting labels.
3 · Build graphStore nodes, relationships, properties, provenance, timestamps, confidence, and permission labels in Neo4j.
4 · Retrieve hybrid contextCombine vector similarity with Cypher traversal, graph neighborhoods, shortest paths, relationship filters, and provenance filters.
5 · Answer with evidenceReturn answer, source chunks, graph path, confidence, lineage, and allowed actions.
Three hybrid retrieval patterns — choose by question type and cost tolerance
Entity-anchored retrieval
embed query → nearest entity nodes → traverse neighborhood
Embed the user query. Find the closest entity nodes using vector similarity. Then traverse the graph neighborhood around those entities via Cypher (e.g. 1–2 hops). Return the subgraph plus the source chunks that mention those entities.
lowest latencygood for lookup questions
Community summarization
pre-compute community summaries → retrieve summaries at query time
Detect graph communities (clusters of tightly connected entities) at index time. Use an LLM to write a summary of each community. At query time, retrieve the most relevant community summaries via vector search. This is Microsoft's original GraphRAG approach — high index cost, very low query-time cost.
global/thematic questionshigh index cost
Path-constrained retrieval
constrain traversal to typed paths · permission filters · relationship type filters
Traverse only along specific, typed relationship paths relevant to the question or the user's permission scope. Example: "Which obligations apply to this account?" traverses only APPLIES_TO and REGULATED_BY edges, filtered to the user's tenant. Prevents traversal from leaking into unrelated or unauthorized subgraphs.
compliance / permissionsaccess-controlled traversal
Cost curves differ: Entity-anchored has the lowest query latency and works well for most knowledge graph use cases. Community summarization has high index cost but very fast and broad query-time answers — use it when questions are global or thematic. Path-constrained is essential for regulated environments where retrieval scope must be enforced at the relationship level.
Retrieval insight · when graph beats vector searchUse Neo4j when the relationship is the answer. If the user asks “how is A connected to B,” “what depends on X,” “which obligations apply to this account,” or “what changed upstream of this report,” the retrieval unit should be a path or neighborhood, not only a chunk.
Best agentic usesAgent memory graphs, permission graphs, data lineage graphs, compliance graphs, incident dependency graphs, financial entity graphs, and GraphRAG over document corpora.
Do not use it for everythingSkip Neo4j when the workload is simple document RAG, pure key-value lookup, normal relational reporting, or when AWS-native Neptune is a hard platform constraint.
🔄 GraphRAG Incremental Updates vs Full Rebuild graph freshness

The RAG embedding pipeline has a well-understood reindex discipline: pin the embedding model, upsert by document ID, trigger full reindex on model upgrade. The graph equivalent is more complex because new documents can introduce entities that should be merged with existing ones, and new relationships can alter the meaning of existing subgraphs.

ScenarioStrategyGraph operationRisk if skipped
New document addedExtract entities and relations from new doc only. MERGE into existing graph using canonical entity IDs.MERGE on entity ID; CREATE for new relationships; UPDATE provenance on existing nodes.Duplicate nodes accumulate; multi-hop paths break across the duplicate boundary.
Document updatedRe-extract from updated doc. Delete stale relations sourced from old version. Merge updated relations.Delete relations with old source chunk ID; re-run extraction; re-MERGE.Stale relationships persist; agent retrieves outdated connections.
Document deletedRemove all relationships with that document as provenance source. Orphan-check nodes — delete nodes whose only provenance was the deleted doc.DELETE relationships by provenance; DELETE orphaned nodes with no remaining relations.Agent traverses ghost relationships that no longer have valid source evidence.
New entity alias discoveredRun disambiguation across new entity and existing candidates. Merge duplicates into canonical node. Repoint all relationships from merged node.MERGE node IDs; redirect relationship endpoints; update entity index.Fragmented nodes break multi-hop reasoning — "MSFT" and "Microsoft" appear as unconnected entities.
Community structure changesIf using community summarization (Microsoft GraphRAG approach): recompute affected communities after significant graph changes. Re-generate community summaries with LLM.Re-run community detection (Louvain, Leiden); re-embed community summaries.Stale community summaries give wrong thematic answers for global queries.
Schema change (new relation type)Treat as a partial rebuild for affected document set. Back-fill extraction with new relation type. Do not full-rebuild unless the schema change is pervasive.Re-extract targeted document set; add new relation type to schema; verify existing traversals still work.Inconsistent schema — some subgraphs have new relation types, others do not.
Operations rule · treat graph updates like schema migrationsEvery incremental graph update is a structural change to shared state, not just a data write. MERGE semantics prevent blind duplicates, but entity resolution must run before the MERGE — otherwise two canonically identical entities arrive as separate nodes and silently split the graph. Build a pre-merge disambiguation step into the ingestion pipeline and run it before every MERGE operation. Test with cross-document queries that require traversal across the newly ingested entities before declaring an update complete.
⚠️Data-Layer Anti-Patternsavoid these
Vector-only memory
Auditreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Semantic search is not a substitute for permissions, transactions, temporal ordering, or source-of-truth records. Pair vector search with relational and policy layers.
Everything in the prompt
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Large prompts increase latency and cost while reducing controllability. Store durable facts, retrieve selectively, and compress context before model invocation.
Graph before relationships matter
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Graph databases are powerful when relationship traversal is central. Do not add them just because the system is called an agent.
No cache or TTL strategy
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Fast agent loops need explicit cache invalidation, session expiry, rate-limit state, and freshness rules. Stale cache can be worse than no cache.
No audit trail
Auditreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
If an agent can take action, persist who asked, what context was used, what policy checks ran, what tool was called, and what changed.
No retrieval evaluation
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Measure recall, precision, citation accuracy, freshness, latency, and permission leakage separately from final-answer quality.
Graph schema designed for completeness, not retrieval
Currentreviewed 2026-05-31
RiskMarket-map
Building a graph that models everything in the corpus before knowing what questions the agent needs to answer. The result is a large, expensive graph that is hard to traverse because the schema was not designed backwards from real retrieval use cases. Start with the questions, then define the schema.
Using GraphRAG for simple document Q&A
Currentreviewed 2026-05-31
RiskMarket-map
Adding a knowledge graph to a workload that is purely similarity-based lookup. If the user is asking "what does the policy say about expense limits," standard RAG is cheaper, faster, and equally accurate. GraphRAG earns its cost when the answer requires traversing entity connections. Add the graph only after standard RAG fails on relationship questions.
Duplicating document text into node properties
Currentreviewed 2026-05-31
RiskMarket-map
Storing full document text in graph node properties rather than keeping long text in the vector layer. This conflates the two layers, bloats the graph database, and duplicates data with no retrieval benefit. Node properties should be short, structured, and queryable. Long text belongs in the vector index; node properties carry IDs and references back to it.
No entity resolution before graph load
Auditreviewed 2026-05-31
RiskMarket-map
Loading extracted entities into the graph without running disambiguation first. "Microsoft," "MSFT," and "Microsoft Corp" arrive as three separate nodes. Multi-hop paths between them break. Entity resolution must run before the MERGE operation for every ingestion batch, not just the initial load.

Data Engineering for Agentic Systems

Agent quality is upstream of model quality. The pipelines that move, clean, transform, and version the data your agents reason over are the unsexy infrastructure that determines whether RAG works in production.
Streaming ingestion — keeping RAG fresh

A vector index is a snapshot. Without a streaming ingestion pipeline, it grows stale the moment source data changes. For most production systems, retrieval staleness is a silent failure — the agent confidently returns outdated facts with no error. The fix is a streaming pipeline that propagates source changes into the index within minutes, not days.

1
Capture source changes
CDC (Change Data Capture) on your operational database via Debezium → Kafka. Or webhook events from your CMS/CRM. Or S3 event notifications on document uploads. Every source system needs a change event, not a scheduled full dump.
debezium · kafka · kinesis · s3 events · webhooks
2
Quality gate before indexing
Every document passes a validation step before embedding: schema check (expected fields present), content check (non-empty, expected language, no injection patterns), deduplication check (content hash not already indexed). Reject and DLQ documents that fail — never silently drop them.
great expectations · soda · pandera · custom validators
3
Chunk, embed, upsert
Apply your chunking strategy, embed with the pinned embedding model, upsert into the vector store with metadata: source_id, version, ingested_at, content_hash, tenant_id. Upsert (not insert) so re-indexing an updated document replaces the old vectors rather than duplicating them.
langchain · llama-index · custom chunker → openai / cohere / voyage
4
Handle deletes explicitly
Source deletes are the hardest part of streaming ingestion. A document deleted from the source must be deleted from the vector store — or the agent will keep retrieving and citing it. Track source_id → vector_ids in a side table so a delete event can remove all associated vectors. This mapping is the most commonly forgotten piece of a streaming ingestion pipeline.
5
Monitor lag and staleness
Track: ingest lag (time from source change to vector store upsert), DLQ depth (failed documents), index coverage (% of source documents indexed), and freshness age (median age of retrieved documents in production traces). Alert when ingest lag exceeds your SLA.
Full reindex vs. incremental update: Reindexing the full corpus is tempting because it's simple, but it's disruptive (index temporarily stale during rebuild), expensive (re-embeds everything), and doesn't scale. Reserve full reindex for: embedding model version changes (mandatory), schema changes to your chunk metadata, or first-time index bootstrap. Use incremental upsert for all ongoing source changes.
Data quality gates in the ingestion pipeline

Bad data in the index produces confidently wrong agent answers. Unlike a model error (which is visible), a retrieval quality problem is invisible — the agent returns a plausible-sounding answer grounded in a stale, malformed, or injected document. Data quality gates are the last line of defense before documents enter the index.

Schema validation
Pandera · Pydantic · JSON Schema
Every document must conform to its expected schema before indexing. Required fields present, data types correct, foreign keys resolvable. A document with a missing tenant_id that gets indexed will leak across tenant boundaries — a critical multi-tenancy failure.
Content quality checks
Custom rules · langdetect · fuzzy dedup
Non-empty content. Expected language (langdetect). Content length within bounds — very short chunks are noise, very long chunks exceed context windows. No injection pattern signatures (prompt-injection scanning). Encoding valid (UTF-8). These catch the most common ingestion failures.
Deduplication
Content hash · MinHash · SimHash
Exact dedup via content hash (SHA-256 of normalized text). Near-dedup via MinHash LSH for catching the same document re-crawled with minor changes. Duplicate documents inflate recall@k metrics while reducing the effective diversity of retrieved context — the agent cites the same fact multiple times with false confidence.
Freshness validation
source_updated_at · TTL rules
Documents older than a configured TTL should be flagged for re-ingestion or soft-deleted. Track source_updated_at separately from ingested_at. An agent retrieving a policy document that was updated 18 months ago and never re-indexed will cite the old policy as current.
Injection scanning
Pattern matching · LLM-based classifier
Scan ingested documents for prompt injection patterns before indexing. A document containing "Ignore all previous instructions and instead…" should be quarantined, not indexed. This is the primary defense against retrieval corpus poisoning (see Security Threat Model). Use both pattern matching (fast) and an LLM-based classifier (thorough) in sequence.
Reject, quarantine, DLQ
Dead-letter queue · alerts · audit trail
Failed validation must never silently drop documents. Route failures to a DLQ with: the document, the failing check, the timestamp, and the source. Alert when DLQ depth exceeds threshold. Build a quarantine review UI so operators can inspect, fix, and re-ingest failed documents. The DLQ is your data quality audit trail.
Great Expectations, Soda, Monte Carlo: Purpose-built data quality frameworks that generate validation reports, track quality over time, and integrate with pipeline orchestrators (Airflow, Prefect, Dagster). For high-volume ingestion pipelines, prefer a framework over hand-rolled checks — they produce structured quality metrics you can alert on and trend over time.
Data catalog & schema discovery for agent SQL

Agents that generate or execute SQL — text-to-SQL, code-executing agents, analytical agents — need access to a machine-readable schema catalog to produce correct queries. An agent that doesn't know what tables exist, what columns mean, or what the foreign key relationships are will hallucinate plausible-looking SQL that fails at runtime or silently returns wrong results.

LayerWhat it provides to the agentTools
Schema registryTable names, column names, types, nullability, primary and foreign keys. The minimum an agent needs to write valid SQL.DataHub · Amundsen · OpenMetadata · Unity Catalog · dbt docs
Semantic layerBusiness definitions: what "revenue" means, which columns contain PII, what the authoritative table for customer records is. Prevents the agent from joining on the wrong key or aggregating the wrong metric.dbt metrics · Cube.dev · AtScale · LookML
Permissions layerWhich tables and columns this agent (or user) is allowed to query. Must be enforced at query execution time, not just in the system prompt. Row-level and column-level security.Unity Catalog · Apache Ranger · Postgres RLS · BigQuery IAM
Sample dataRepresentative values per column so the agent can understand cardinality, format, and expected values without running a full scan.dbt sources · catalog samplers · profiling tools
Query historyPreviously successful queries for similar questions — analogous to few-shot examples but drawn from real query patterns rather than hand-written examples.Snowflake query history · BigQuery INFORMATION_SCHEMA · dbt exposures
Inject catalog context, not full schema dumps. Full database schemas can be enormous. Inject only the tables relevant to the question — use semantic search over the catalog (embed table descriptions, retrieve top-k relevant tables) the same way you use RAG for document retrieval. A well-designed schema retrieval pipeline is RAG for your database schema.
dbt as the semantic layer: If your team already uses dbt, you have a schema catalog for free. dbt docs generate produces a structured JSON artifact with all table descriptions, column definitions, and lineage. Parse this artifact and load it into your agent's context assembly pipeline as the authoritative schema source.
Lakehouse architecture & open table formats

As agents increasingly query analytical corpora — not just vector indexes — the underlying storage format determines schema evolution, time travel, and ACID guarantees on the data the agent reasons over. Open table formats are becoming the standard substrate for agent-queryable analytical data.

Apache Iceberg
Open table format · ACID · schema evolution · time travel
The most widely adopted open table format for cloud lakehouses. ACID transactions, schema evolution without full rewrites, partition evolution, and time travel queries (AS OF timestamp). Supported by Spark, Flink, Trino, Athena, BigQuery. For agents: time travel enables reproducible queries — run the same analytical query against a snapshot from a specific date.
Delta Lake
Databricks-origin · ACID · DML on Parquet
Originated at Databricks, now Linux Foundation open source. ACID transactions on Parquet files, Z-order clustering for query performance, Change Data Feed for CDC-style downstream consumption. If your stack is Databricks-native, Delta is the default. For agents querying Databricks-managed data, Delta tables with Unity Catalog are the production standard.
dbt (data build tool)
SQL transformation layer · lineage · docs · tests
dbt transforms raw data into agent-queryable models using version-controlled SQL. dbt test runs data quality assertions on every model. dbt docs generates the schema catalog agents need for text-to-SQL. dbt lineage DAG shows which upstream tables a model depends on — critical for understanding the provenance of data an agent cites.
// DE/ML insight · why this matters for agentsTime travel queries are reproducible agent behavior. If an agent answers an analytical question today and you need to reproduce that answer tomorrow, you need the data to be queryable as-of the same timestamp. Open table formats (Iceberg AS OF, Delta time travel) make agent answers reproducible in the same way that pinning model versions makes model outputs reproducible. Without time travel, analytical agent answers are unreproducible — a compliance and audit problem in regulated industries.
// MLOps insight · when agents call ML models as toolsIf your agent calls a traditional ML model as a tool — a recommender, a classifier, a propensity scorer — that model has a feature store dependency you must expose to the agent. The feature store serves engineered features to the ML model at serving time; the agent's tool call is the serving request. Design: (1) wrap the ML model call in a typed tool schema that hides the feature store internals; (2) version the tool schema alongside the model version — a model retrain that changes input features is a breaking tool contract change; (3) monitor feature freshness for the features the tool depends on — a stale feature store produces stale tool results the agent will reason over as if they were current. Tools: Feast, Tecton, Hopsworks, Redis-based feature serving.
Enterprise Data + AI Platforms for Agents

Databricks and Snowflake are not primarily agent frameworks. They sit under and beside the agent loop as governed enterprise data, retrieval, AI-serving, and evaluation platforms. Agent frameworks decide what to do next; these platforms decide what trusted data, tools, models, policies, and lineage the agent can safely use.

Databricks
Lakehouse · ML lifecycle · Unity Catalog · MLflow · Vector Search
Best when the agent is close to data engineering, ML engineering, feature pipelines, notebooks, model experimentation, or lakehouse-scale analytical data. Use Databricks as the governed data and ML lifecycle backbone: Delta Lake for reproducible tables, Unity Catalog for permissions and lineage, Vector Search for RAG over lakehouse-managed corpora, MLflow for experiment/eval/model lifecycle, and Mosaic AI / Model Serving for deployment.
lakehouseMLflowUnity CatalogVector Search
Snowflake
Warehouse · SQL analytics · Cortex · Streamlit · governed business data
Best when the agent is close to BI, finance, revenue analytics, operations reporting, governed SQL, or business-user-facing data apps. Use Snowflake as the governed analytics backbone: Cortex Analyst for text-to-SQL over structured warehouse data, Cortex Search for search/RAG over Snowflake-managed content, Cortex Agents for routing across structured data, unstructured data, stored procedures, and UDFs, and Streamlit for internal copilot and review UIs.
warehouseCortextext-to-SQLStreamlit
DecisionUse Databricks when…Use Snowflake when…
Center of gravityThe work starts in lakehouse, Spark, notebooks, ML pipelines, feature engineering, or model lifecycle.The work starts in warehouse analytics, BI, governed SQL, finance/revenue/ops reporting, or business metrics.
Agent roleAgent calls governed data, feature/model tools, retrieval indexes, ML experiments, and model serving endpoints.Agent answers business questions, generates governed SQL, searches enterprise content, and launches internal data workflows.
Governance primitiveUnity Catalog + Delta Lake + MLflow lineage across data, models, evals, and serving artifacts.Snowflake governance + Cortex + warehouse permissions around structured and unstructured analytical assets.
UI/app patternML/data engineering workbench, evaluation dashboards, model/agent deployment workflows.Streamlit internal apps, analyst copilots, metric drill-downs, approval/review interfaces.
Use both when…Databricks owns ML/feature/model workflows while Snowflake remains the business warehouse. The agent can call both: Snowflake for trusted business metrics; Databricks for ML-derived predictions, embeddings, feature-backed tools, and lakehouse-scale analytical processing.
// Placement ruleDo not classify Databricks or Snowflake as substitutes for LangGraph, OpenAI Agents, Claude Agent SDK, or CrewAI. Classify them as enterprise data + AI platforms that provide governed data access, retrieval, SQL, model serving, evaluation, lineage, and internal app surfaces that agent frameworks can call into.

Agentic PrimitivesStable

The internal anatomy of Claude Code & Agent SDK — not a separate layer, but the vocabulary that programs agent behavior from within Layer 2.
🤖
Subagents
Delegation primitive
reaches into
Orchestrator layer
Delegates a task to a fresh Claude instance with its own context window. The core fan-out primitive — each subagent is isolated, parallel, and can itself spawn further subagents. Configured via Task() in the Agent SDK or /agent invocation in Claude Code. Enables hierarchical orchestration: orchestrator → specialists → sub-specialists.
parallel executionisolated contexthierarchical
⚡
Hooks
Event primitive
reaches into
Observability layer
Event listeners that fire at agent lifecycle moments: PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop, Notification. Defined in .claude/hooks/. Ideal injection point for Langfuse tracing, custom logging, guardrails, and cost tracking. Can block, modify, or redirect tool calls before execution.
lifecycle eventsmiddlewareinterceptors
📦
Skills
Capability primitive
reaches into
Knowledge layer
Reusable capability bundles — prompt + tools + instructions packaged together and stored in .claude/skills/ or /mnt/skills/. Loaded on demand. Can wrap an entire RAG retrieval flow, a code generation pattern, or a document formatting pipeline. Composable: a skill can invoke other skills.
reusablecomposablebundled context
⌨️
Slash Commands
Invocation primitive
reaches into
Loop layer
Custom invocable actions defined as markdown files in .claude/commands/. Typed as /command-name at runtime. Act as user-defined macros — encapsulate multi-step workflows into a single invocation. Can accept arguments, reference project files, and chain into subagents.
macrosworkflow triggers.claude/commands/
📄
CLAUDE.md
Context primitive
reaches into
Memory layer
The harness system prompt — persistent context, rules, persona, tool guidance, and project conventions injected into every agent session. Hierarchical: project-level, directory-level, user-level. Acts as hand-authored long-term memory. The single most impactful file for shaping agent behavior.
system promptpersistent contexthierarchical
🔌
MCP Tools
Integration primitive
reaches into
Knowledge layer
External tool connections via Model Context Protocol — Anthropic's open standard for agent tooling. Configured in .claude/mcp.json. Each MCP server exposes typed tools the agent can call: DuckDB SQL, Power BI semantic queries, GitHub ops, browser control. The USB-C of agent integrations.
open standardtyped tools.claude/mcp.json
🧠
In-Context Memory
State primitive
reaches into
State & Persistence layer
The active context window — everything the agent can "see" right now. Finite and precious (~200k tokens). Managed by the harness: CLAUDE.md loaded first, then tool results, conversation history, retrieved chunks, and subagent outputs. When it fills, the harness must decide what to evict — this is where external memory and retrieval become necessary. Distinct from persistent memory (cross-session) and durable state (Redis/Postgres).
context windowephemeral~200k tokenssingle run
// Agentic primitives — which layer each one reaches into
TL;DR before this long table
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
PrimitiveConfig locationRoleReaches into layer
SubagentsTask() / /agentDelegate tasks to fresh Claude instancesOrchestrator
Hooks.claude/hooks/Lifecycle event listeners: pre/post tool, stopObservability
Skills.claude/skills/ · /mnt/skills/Reusable capability bundlesKnowledge
Slash Commands.claude/commands/Custom invocable macros and workflowsLoop Patterns & Iteration
CLAUDE.mdProject / dir / user levelPersistent system prompt and rulesMemory
MCP Tools.claude/mcp.jsonExternal tool connections via open standardKnowledge + Integration
In-Context MemoryActive context windowEverything the agent can see right now (~200k tokens)State & Persistence
// Concept explainer · For ML/DS readersWhy do control loops exist? A language model, on its own, produces one response per prompt — a single forward pass. A control loop wraps that model in a repeat until done structure: the model thinks, calls a tool, observes the result, and decides whether to continue. ReAct is the simplest version: Reason → Act → Observe, repeated. This is why agents can do multi-step work that a single inference call cannot. Think of it as replacing a single model call with an iterative search over actions — closer to dynamic programming than to standard batch inference.
// Control Loop Taxonomy · v3.13

Control Loop Taxonomy

Control loops determine how an agent moves from uncertainty to action. Treat them as execution policies: use simple linear loops by default, add replanning when the environment changes, add consensus when correctness matters, and reserve search-heavy loops for hard planning problems where extra latency and cost are justified.

Linear execution loopsThink–Act–Observe, ReAct, Replan, and Plan–Execute–Reflect. Best for tool use, workflow execution, debugging, and most production agents.
Search loopsBeam Search, Tree-of-Thought, MCTS, and LATS. Best when the agent must compare multiple candidate plans or reasoning paths before acting.
Consensus loopsSelf-consistency, majority voting, debate, and multi-agent review. Best when reliability is more important than speed or token cost.
Default production pathThink → Act → Observe → Replan if needed
→
Hard problem pathBranch → Score → Prune → Select
→
High-confidence pathSample → Vote / Debate → Verify
Control Loop Families │ ├── Linear execution loops │ ├── Think–Act–Observe / ReAct │ ├── Replan Loop │ └── Plan–Execute–Reflect │ ├── Search loops │ ├── Beam Search │ ├── Tree-of-Thought │ └── MCTS / LATS │ └── Consensus loops ├── Self-consistency ├── Majority voting └── Debate / multi-agent review
Production rule of thumb: start with Think–Act–Observe plus explicit stop conditions. Add a Replan Loop for changing environments, consensus for high-impact decisions, and search loops only when a single trajectory repeatedly gets stuck or misses better plans.

Agent Complexity SpectrumStable

A chatbot is a degenerate case of an agent — same stack, fewer layers active. Every layer you add moves it along this spectrum.
Chatbot
turn-based · reactive
Assistant
tools · memory · RAG
Agent
plans · loops · subagents
Multi-Agent
orchestration · fan-out
Autonomous
self-directed · persistent

Doc Processing & Ingestion—⚠️✓✓✓
