Treat provider-forced upgrades as a production incident risk and treat A/B testing across model versions as a first-class operational capability.
// MLOps insight · shadow mode for agent upgradesShadow mode is the safest way to validate a new agent version before any user-facing cutover. Route live traffic to both current and candidate agent versions simultaneously. Serve only the current version's response to users; collect both responses for offline evaluation. Compare: answer quality (LLM-as-judge), tool call patterns, latency, cost, error rate. Promote only when shadow eval passes your quality threshold — zero users were exposed to the experiment. This is identical to shadow mode in ML model serving: a traffic splitter at the API gateway, a parallel execution path, and a comparison eval pipeline. Unlike A/B testing, shadow mode has zero user risk. Essential before any model version upgrade or major prompt change in a production system.
Section TL;DR
  • Most spend is in input tokens and embedding pipeline - optimize those before infrastructure.
  • Semantic cache threshold is a precision-recall tradeoff, not a free win - tune on a held-out query set.
  • Route by complexity: simple to cheapest model; standard RAG to mid-tier; reasoning to frontier.
  • Add cost-per-task assertions to your eval suite - cost regression is a regression even if quality passes.

Cost Architecture

Cost checklist
  • Set token, tool-call, retrieval, and model-routing budgets before scale testing.
  • Measure cache hit rate, fallback rate, retry amplification, and expensive-model escalation.
  • Prefer cheaper models only after evals prove task success and safety remain stable.
The prototype works. The unit economics do not. Cost architecture is the discipline of making production AI systems financially sustainable at scale.
Cost anatomy per request
TL;DR before this long table
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
Cost componentDriverReduction lever
Input tokensSystem prompt + context + conversation history + retrieved docs + user message.Prompt compression, history summarization, tighter retrieval, context budgeting.
Output tokensResponse length. Verbose models or unbounded output windows drive this up fast.Explicit length constraints in prompt. Max output token cap per request tier.
Embedding callsEmbedding every query and every ingested document chunk.Cache query embeddings for semantically similar queries. Batch ingestion calls.
Tool API callsExternal API calls the agent makes — search, database, SaaS tools.Cache deterministic tool results with TTL. Limit tool calls per turn via the tool manifest.
Reranker callsReranking top-k candidates per query.Only invoke reranker when initial retrieval confidence is below threshold. Cache results for repeated queries.
Guardrail / safety model callsInput and output scanning via a separate safety model or API.Run safety checks on a cheaper model. Skip for trusted internal users if risk profile allows.
Caching strategy
Prompt caching
Cache the static prefix of the system prompt at the provider level (Anthropic supports prompt caching with cache_control). Reduces input token cost significantly for long system prompts reused across requests.
Semantic cache
Cache full LLM responses keyed by embedding similarity to the query. GPTCache or custom Redis + vector similarity. Cache hit rate depends heavily on query distribution — measure before assuming it helps.
KV / deterministic cache
Cache tool results and retrieval results keyed by exact query hash. TTL-based invalidation. High hit rate for repeated identical queries. Does not help for novel queries.
// ML/DS insight · cache threshold is a precision-recall hyperparameterThe similarity threshold that triggers a semantic cache hit is a precision-recall tradeoff you need to tune, not set arbitrarily. A lower threshold returns more cache hits (higher recall) but risks returning a cached answer to a query that is similar-but-not-identical — a precision problem. Measure cache precision (% of hits where the cached answer is correct for the new query) alongside the cache hit rate. A cache with a 40% hit rate and 60% precision is actively degrading answer quality. Treat the threshold as a hyperparameter: tune it on a held-out query set, plot hit rate vs. precision as you vary it, and choose the operating point that meets your quality floor. Re-tune after significant changes to your user query distribution.
Model routing by cost tier
Not every request needs the frontier model. Route by complexity: simple classification and routing decisions → cheapest fast model (Haiku, GPT-4o-mini). Standard Q&A, summarization, RAG responses → mid-tier model (Sonnet). Complex multi-step reasoning, orchestrator decisions, long-context synthesis → frontier model (Opus, GPT-4o). Implement routing as a classifier model or rules-based system. Measure quality impact of routing decisions in evals — routing that degrades quality is not saving money, it is losing users.
// ML/DS insight · routing as a learned classifierRule-based routing is fast to build and brittle to maintain — consider training a routing classifier. As query complexity grows, a rule set becomes a decision tree that degrades under distribution shift. The scalable alternative is a lightweight intent classifier: a small fast model predicts the complexity tier and routes the request before the main model call. Build the training set from production traces labelled with route outcome and downstream quality. Deploy the classifier at the gateway — it adds single-digit milliseconds but makes routing accuracy measurable, improvable, and drift-detectable over time. Track routing accuracy the same way you track any model in production: periodic eval against labelled samples, alerting on distribution shift, and retraining when accuracy drops.
Cost governance
Hard cost caps
Enforce per-user, per-session, and per-day token budgets at the gateway. When a budget is exhausted, return a graceful "limit reached" response. Reset budgets on schedule.
Cost-aware testing
Add a cost-per-task assertion to your eval suite. If a test case that should cost $0.02 now costs $0.18, that is a regression even if quality metrics pass. Track cost per task over time in CI.
Tenant cost quotas
Track cost per tenant_id. Enforce quotas matching the tenant's pricing tier. Alert the tenant before they hit their quota. Alert your team when a tenant's cost suddenly spikes.
Cost modeling before scaling
Before scaling traffic by 10x, model the cost impact. Build a cost estimator from token usage distribution and request volume. Run this as part of capacity planning, not as a post-launch surprise.

Deployment Pipeline (CI/CD for AI Systems)

AI systems need a CI/CD pipeline that handles three things traditional pipelines do not: prompt changes, model version changes, and behavioral regressions that cannot be detected by unit tests alone.
What goes in version control
TL;DR before this long table
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
ArtifactStorageReview requirement
System promptsGit (as .md or .txt files in prompts/ directory)PR review + eval run must pass before merge. Treat as code.
Model version pinsGit (in config/models.yaml or equivalent)PR review. Change triggers automatic eval run in CI.
Tool schemasGit (JSON Schema models)PR review. Schema changes are breaking changes — version them.
Guardrail rulesGit (policy files in policies/)PR review. Safety rule changes require safety eval run to pass.
Golden eval datasetGit LFS or artifact store (e.g. DVC, LangSmith datasets)Modifying expected outputs requires justification. Adding cases is always safe.
Retrieval index configGit (chunking strategy, embedding model, HNSW params)Index config changes trigger reindex pipeline in staging before prod promotion.
CI/CD pipeline stages for AI systems
STAGE 1
Lint & validate
Prompt schema validation, JSON Schema lint, policy file syntax, model pin format check.
STAGE 2
Unit tests
Tool schema validation, output format parsing, guardrail logic, state machine tests. No LLM calls.
STAGE 3
Safety evals
Red-team prompts, injection probes, content policy. Zero tolerance — any failure blocks merge.
STAGE 4
Quality evals
Golden dataset run. Quality, retrieval, tool-use metrics vs. baseline. Regression threshold blocks merge.
STAGE 5
Cost check
Cost-per-task vs. budget. Token usage vs. baseline. Flag if P95 cost exceeds threshold.
STAGE 6
Deploy & canary
Staging deploy → 48h soak → canary (5%) → gate on live metrics → full rollout.
Feature flags for AI features
Every significant AI capability should be behind a feature flag. This enables: targeted rollout to specific user segments or tenants, instant kill switch without a code deploy, A/B testing of different prompts or model versions, gradual traffic migration, and per-tenant capability control. The flag state should be configurable without a code change. Changes to flag state in production should be logged with author and timestamp. The kill switch is the most important flag — verify it works before every launch.
Prompt change management
Treat prompt changes as releases
A prompt change is a behavior change. It goes through the same PR, review, and eval gate process as a code change. "Quick prompt fix" is a deployment risk, not a shortcut. Every prompt change must have a before/after eval comparison.
Keep a prompt changelog
Document what changed, why, what behavior it was intended to fix, and what evals it passed. When a production incident traces back to a prompt change, this changelog is the forensic record that distinguishes intentional change from accidental regression.
The non-negotiable pipeline rule: no change to system prompt, model version, guardrail policy, retrieval index config, or tool schema reaches production without a passing eval run. The eval run is the single source of truth — not developer confidence, not a demo, not a staging spot-check.

Canonical Production ArchitectureStable

The canonical request path for a production chatbot or agent. Every component is independently observable, versioned, permissioned, and rollbackable.
Reference request flow
1. User / Channel
Web, mobile, Slack, Teams, voice, support desk.
2. API Gateway
Rate limits, auth, tenant routing, request IDs.
3. Conversation Service
Session, history, message state, retention policy.
4. Agent Runtime
Plan, route, call tools, retrieve, respond.
5. Response Renderer
Streaming, citations, cards, actions, handoff.
Policy Gate
PII, prompt-injection, content, approval policy.
Retrieval Layer
ACL-aware search, rerank, citations, freshness.
Model Gateway
Routing, fallback, budgets, model pins, logging.
Tool Execution
Allowlist, schema validation, sandbox, approvals.
State Stores
Postgres, Redis, vector DB, audit log, warehouse.
Cross-cutting control plane: tracing, eval capture, feedback, metrics, cost accounting, security audit, feature flags, release versions, incident alerts, and rollback controls wrap every request path.
PatternRequired additionsProduction rule
Basic chatbotAuth, session store, prompt versions, model gateway, feedback, observability.No production launch without traceable prompt/model versions and human escalation.
RAG assistantIngestion, ACL-aware retrieval, source citations, no-answer behavior, freshness checks.No chunk reaches the prompt until tenant and document permissions are verified.
Tool-using agentTool registry, schemas, permission checks, dry run, approval flow, idempotency keys, audit logs.No write/action tool executes without explicit risk tier, owner, rollback plan, and audit record.

Async Job Queue ArchitectureCurrent

Any agent run longer than ~30 seconds needs a job queue under it. Blocking HTTP connections for long-running agents is the most common production architecture mistake for agentic systems.
Why synchronous HTTP fails for agents

A synchronous agent endpoint — POST /run → wait → response — works fine for runs under ~10 seconds. Beyond that: load balancers time out, mobile clients disconnect, server restarts lose in-flight runs, and a single stuck tool call blocks the entire response. The production pattern is always async: accept the job, return a job ID immediately, run the agent in a worker, poll or push the result.

1. API Gateway
POST /run → validates, enqueues, returns job_id immediately (202 Accepted)
→
2. Job Queue
SQS / BullMQ / Redis Streams / Temporal. Durable. At-least-once delivery. Dead-letter queue for failures.
→
3. Worker Pool
Agent runtime. Pulls jobs, runs the agent loop, writes state to checkpoint store. Scales by queue depth.
→
4. Result Store
Redis / DynamoDB / Postgres. Stores job status (pending → running → done/failed), result, trace_id.
Queue optionBest forDurabilityDurable execution?
AWS SQS + Lambda/ECSAWS-native, serverless workersManaged, at-least-onceNo — need external state store
BullMQ (Redis)Node.js stacks, priorities, low latencyRedis AOF persistenceNo — need checkpoint pattern
Celery (Redis/RabbitMQ)Python stacks, ML workloadsBroker-dependentNo — need checkpoint pattern
TemporalLong-running, multi-step, HITL pause/resumeFull event sourcingYes — built-in durable execution
LangGraph + checkpointerLangGraph agents needing pause/resumePostgres / Redis backendYes — graph state persisted per step
Result delivery patterns: (1) Polling — client polls GET /run/{job_id}/status until done; simple but chatty. (2) Webhook — server POSTs result to a client URL on completion; good for server-to-server. (3) SSE stream — client opens GET /run/{job_id}/stream and receives token-by-token events; best UX for chat agents. Use (3) for human-facing agents, (2) for API integrations, (1) for simple internal tooling.
Dead-letter queue is not optional. Every job queue needs a DLQ. Failed agent runs must land somewhere observable — not silently disappear. Configure: max receive count (retries before DLQ), DLQ depth alarm (alert when DLQ > 0), and a replay mechanism (re-enqueue DLQ jobs after fixing root cause). A DLQ is your agent system’s catch-all error handler and audit trail for failures.
Agent state checkpointing & resume

Checkpointing persists agent state after every step so a crashed or paused agent can resume from the last checkpoint rather than restarting from scratch. Essential for HITL approval gates (pause mid-run, wait for human, resume), cost control (restart only failed steps), and long multi-step runs (survive worker restarts).

1
Define checkpoint schema
Minimum fields: run_id, step_index, graph_state (serialized), tool_results_so_far, status (running / awaiting_human / done / failed), created_at. Store in Postgres or Redis with TTL.
2
Write after every tool call
Before executing the next step, persist current state. If the worker crashes between steps, the next worker picks up from the last persisted checkpoint — the write-ahead log pattern from databases applied to agent execution.
3
HITL pause/resume via checkpoint status
When the agent reaches an approval gate: write checkpoint with status: awaiting_human, surface the proposed action to the reviewer, block until status changes to approved or rejected. Worker resumes by loading checkpoint and continuing from step_index + 1.
4
LangGraph checkpointer or Temporal
LangGraph’s built-in checkpointer (Postgres or Redis backend) handles steps 1–3 automatically. Temporal’s workflow engine handles durable execution at the infrastructure level — crashes are transparent to application code. Use Temporal for multi-agent coordination across services; LangGraph checkpointer for single-agent pause/resume.
langgraph checkpointer · temporal workflows · postgres · redis
// SWE insight · structured logging & trace correlationEvery log line from an agent run should carry trace_id, run_id, step_index, tenant_id, and model_version as structured JSON fields — not embedded in a message string. Propagate trace_id from the API gateway through every service, tool call, and retrieval query in W3C TraceContext format. Index logs in a system that supports field-based queries (CloudWatch Logs Insights, Datadog, OpenSearch). The operational question “what did this specific agent run do and why did it fail?” should be answerable from logs within 60 seconds. Without structured logging, agent debugging requires correlating traces, logs, and tool call records by hand — an expensive, error-prone process at 3am during an incident.

Agent Architecture PatternsStable

How agent systems are organized before any specific loop executes. Patterns describe the system shape; loops describe repeated execution inside that shape.
Architecture layer · v3.26
Pattern first, loop second
Use this section to choose the shape of the agent system before choosing ReAct, Plan–Execute–Reflect, verifier loops, search loops, or consensus loops. A good production system usually combines a deterministic workflow shell, a small number of agentic nodes, explicit risk gates, and measurable verifier checkpoints.
TL;DR: Start with a Single-Agent Tool Loop or Workflow / DAG. Add a Router when requests split into clear domains. Add Planner–Executor when tasks require decomposition. Add Evaluator–Optimizer and Critic / Verifier when quality matters. Add Orchestrator–Worker, Supervisor, Swarm, or Debate only when evals prove the extra coordination improves outcomes enough to justify cost, latency, and operational complexity.
Pattern Taxonomy
Execution patternsShape the main work path.
  • Single-Agent Tool Loop
  • Workflow / DAG
  • Planner–Executor
Routing patternsChoose the right path, skill, model, workflow, or agent.
  • Router
  • Model Router
  • Tool Router
  • Skill Router
Delegation patternsSplit work across specialized agents or workers.
  • Orchestrator–Worker
  • Supervisor
  • Swarm / Peer Agents
Quality patternsImprove or check the output before it reaches users.
  • Evaluator–Optimizer
  • Critic / Verifier
  • Debate / Adversarial Review
Knowledge patternsGround the agent in external knowledge and durable context.
  • Retrieval-Grounded Agent
  • Memory-Augmented Agent
  • Learn–Distill–Persist
Pattern specialization · GraphRAGGraphRAG is Retrieval-Grounded Agent + Knowledge Graph. Standard RAG retrieves chunks. GraphRAG retrieves entities, relationships, paths, neighborhoods, communities, permissions, and provenance. Use Neo4j when Cypher traversal, graph-native entity modeling, vector search over graph entities, and explainable relationship paths should be part of the context pack.
Safety patternsControl autonomy, cost, permissions, and failure handling.
  • Human-in-the-Loop Approval
  • Risk-Gated Action
  • Budget-Gated Autonomy
  • Fallback / Graceful Degradation
Pattern Composition Diagram
Use this as the compact mental model for choosing an agent architecture pattern before choosing the inner execution loop.
User Request
   ↓
Router / Risk Classifier
   ↓
Pattern Selection
   ├─ Simple task → Single-Agent Tool Loop
   ├─ Predictable process → Workflow / DAG
   ├─ Complex task → Planner–Executor
   ├─ Multi-domain task → Orchestrator–Worker
   ├─ High-quality output → Evaluator–Optimizer
   ├─ Knowledge-heavy task → Retrieval-Grounded Agent
   └─ Risky action → HITL / Risk-Gated Action
   ↓
Verifier / Evaluator
   ↓
Memory / Trace / Audit Log
   ↓
Final Answer or Action
1 · Request intakeUser request enters through an app, chat surface, API, CLI, or automated job. Attach tenant, auth, trace, and policy context immediately.
2 · Router / risk classifierClassify intent, domain, data sensitivity, action risk, cost budget, and whether the request needs retrieval, tools, approval, or deterministic workflow.
3 · Pattern selectionChoose Single-Agent, Workflow / DAG, Planner–Executor, Orchestrator–Worker, Evaluator–Optimizer, Retrieval-Grounded, HITL, or Risk-Gated path.
4 · Verify / persist / respondRun evaluator or verifier, persist traces and memory updates, disclose limitations when needed, and return final answer or approved action.
Core Pattern Cards
Single-Agent Tool Loop
default · lowest orchestration overhead
Use when the task is narrow and tools are clear. One agent reasons, calls tools, observes results, and stops. This is the baseline before adding routers, supervisors, or multi-agent coordination.
Workflow / DAG
production default · most auditable
Use when the process has predictable stages such as ingest → classify → retrieve → draft → verify → approve → publish. LLMs appear inside bounded nodes, not as the whole control plane.
Router
domain split · skill selection
Use when requests naturally divide into coding, research, SQL, document editing, retrieval, customer support, or data-analysis paths. Avoid when categories are vague or overlapping.
Planner–Executor
decomposition · task graph
Use when the system must separate strategic planning from action execution. Pair with a verifier and a replan loop when observations invalidate the plan.
Orchestrator–Worker
parallel subtasks · specialist workers
Use when subtasks are separable and outputs can be merged. Do not use it to compensate for vague requirements or weak acceptance criteria.
Supervisor
long-running coordination
Use when multiple agents need monitoring, redirection, policy enforcement, and stop decisions over time. A supervisor manages progress; an orchestrator mainly decomposes and delegates.
Evaluator–Optimizer
quality loop · rubric driven
Use when quality matters more than latency. Generate, evaluate against a rubric, critique, revise, and accept only after threshold checks pass.
Critic / Verifier
grounding · policy · tests
Use when outputs can be checked: SQL safety, citation grounding, code tests, schema validity, tool arguments, security policy, or business rules.
Retrieval-Grounded Agent
private/fresh/cited knowledge
Use when the agent must plan queries, retrieve evidence, read sources, reason over context, cite claims, and verify grounding before answering.
Memory-Augmented Agent
continuity · personalization
Use when the agent needs working memory, episodic traces, semantic memory, user preferences, durable state, or learned project context across sessions.
Human-in-the-Loop Approval
irreversible or external action
Use when actions affect money, infrastructure, users, permissions, production systems, sensitive data, or external communications. Draft first; approve before execute.
Risk-Gated Action
autonomy control
Use when agents have write tools. Low-risk actions may execute; medium-risk actions require dry run or sandbox; high-risk actions require approval; prohibited actions are blocked.
Swarm / Peer Agents
exploration · expensive
Use when independent exploration is valuable, such as multiple research agents or competing implementation proposals. Gate with evals because cost and latency rise quickly.
Debate / Adversarial Review
assumption challenge
Use when subtle failure modes, tradeoffs, or high-stakes assumptions need explicit challenge. Prefer targeted red-team review over open-ended debate.
Fallback / Graceful Degradation
resilience · user-facing systems
Use when tools fail, retrieval is incomplete, models time out, or confidence is low. Retry safely, route to fallback, return partial answer, disclose limitation, and log the failure path.
Pattern Selection Matrix
PatternUse whenAvoid whenProduction default?
Single-Agent Tool LoopTask is narrow and tools are clear.Task needs parallel specialists or strict workflow control.Yes
Workflow / DAGSteps are predictable, auditable, and repeatable.Task needs open-ended exploration.Yes
RouterRequests fall into clear domains, skills, tools, or model tiers.Categories are vague or constantly changing.Yes
Planner–ExecutorTask requires decomposition and plan tracking.Direct execution is simpler and cheaper.Yes
Evaluator–OptimizerQuality matters more than latency and a rubric exists.No acceptance criteria or measurable quality signal exists.Yes
Critic / VerifierOutputs can be checked against tests, schema, policy, citations, or business rules.There is nothing objective to verify.Yes
Retrieval-Grounded AgentNeed private, fresh, cited, or domain-specific knowledge.Answer can be produced safely from existing context.Yes
HITL ApprovalActions are irreversible, external-facing, expensive, or sensitive.Read-only low-risk tasks.Yes
Risk-Gated ActionAgent has write tools or privileged tool access.Agent is read-only.Yes
Orchestrator–WorkerSubtasks are separable and mergeable.Requirements are vague or evaluation is weak.Sometimes
SupervisorLong-running multi-agent work needs monitoring and redirects.One-shot task.Sometimes
Memory-Augmented AgentNeed continuity, personalization, or learned project context.No persistence policy or memory deletion path exists.Sometimes
Swarm / Peer AgentsIndependent exploration materially improves outcomes.Latency, cost, or determinism matters more.Rare
Debate / Adversarial ReviewAssumptions require explicit challenge.Simple factual or transactional task.Rare
Fallback / Graceful DegradationUser-facing systems need resilience under model, tool, or retrieval failure.Internal prototype only.Yes
Pattern anti-patterns: do not add a supervisor because the prompt is unclear; do not add swarms before evals prove benefit; do not use debate when a deterministic verifier would work; do not give a router overlapping categories; do not let memory persist without retention, deletion, and audit rules; do not let agents take write actions without risk classification, scoped credentials, and traceable approval.

GraphRAG Cost ModelCurrent

GraphRAG has a fundamentally different cost curve from standard RAG. The index-build phase is expensive; the query-time phase can be cheaper than standard RAG if community summarization is pre-computed.
Cost comparison: standard RAG vs two GraphRAG approaches
PhaseStandard RAGGraphRAG — traversal approachGraphRAG — community summarization
Corpus ingestionLow — embed chunks, load indexHigh — LLM calls for NER, relation extraction, disambiguation per document chunkVery high — above + community detection + LLM summarization per community
Index storageVector index onlyVector index + graph DB storage + property storage per node/edgeAbove + community summary embeddings
Incremental updateLow — upsert changed doc chunksMedium — re-extract changed docs, disambiguation, re-MERGEHigh if community structure changes — recompute communities + re-summarize
Query — first token latencyLow — ANN search ms rangeMedium — entity vector lookup + Cypher traversal + chunk assemblyLow — community summary lookup via ANN is fast
Query — LLM tokens per requestVariable — depends on retrieved chunk countVariable — depends on graph neighborhood size injected into contextVariable — community summary text may be long
Best cost profile forHigh query volume, broad corpus, similarity-based questionsRelationship questions, compliance, entity resolution — query volume moderateGlobal/thematic questions, small-to-medium corpus, read-heavy workload
Index-build cost rule
Budget approximately 1–3 LLM API calls per chunk for extraction (NER + relation extraction). For a 10,000-document corpus with average 5 chunks per document, expect 50,000–150,000 LLM calls during initial build. Use batch API endpoints and a cheaper extraction model (not your most capable frontier model) to control this cost.
Query-time optimization
Cache traversal results for common entity pairs. Set maximum hop depth (2–3 hops covers most use cases; deeper traversal grows context quadratically). Use path-constrained traversal to limit neighborhood size. Pre-compute and cache community summaries rather than recomputing at query time.

Build-Order Roadmap

A production agent should be assembled in layers. Do not add autonomy until the previous layer is observable, evaluated, and reversible.
TL;DR before this long table
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
PhaseBuildExit criteriaDo not add yet
0 · PrototypeSingle model, static prompt, local UI, manual test questions.Can explain target users, top tasks, success/failure examples.Memory, write tools, autonomy.
1 · Internal chatbotAuth, conversation service, prompt versioning, feedback, tracing, support handoff.Internal users can complete common tasks; traces capture every run.External data actions.
2 · RAG assistantIngestion pipeline, embeddings, reranking, ACL filtering, citations, no-answer policy.Retrieval evals pass; citations are accurate; restricted docs never leak.Autonomous actions.
3 · Tool-using agentTool registry, schemas, dry-run execution, approval workflow, audit log.Tool selection and argument evals pass; every action is reversible or compensatable.Unapproved write actions.
4 · Production hardeningCanary releases, SLO dashboards, kill switches, cost budgets, runbooks, on-call.P95 latency, quality, safety, cost, and escalation metrics meet launch gates.Broad autonomy.
5 · Bounded autonomyLow-risk autonomous actions, policy engine, idempotency, queue execution, monitoring.Incident-free soak period; approvals only relaxed for low-risk, high-confidence actions.High-impact financial, legal, security, or customer-visible actions.
// SWE insight · chaos engineering for agent systemsAgent systems have more failure modes than traditional APIs — which makes chaos engineering more important, not less. Failure modes unique to agents: tool call timeout mid-run (does the agent retry, abort, or loop infinitely?), retrieval returns empty results (does the agent hallucinate or acknowledge uncertainty?), model returns malformed structured output (does parsing fail gracefully?), budget exhausted mid-task (partial result or clean abort?). Test by: injecting tool timeouts, returning empty retrieval results for a % of queries, injecting 429s from the model API, exhausting token budget mid-plan. Measure: graceful degradation, coherent partial results, alerting, state integrity. Agent systems that have never been chaos-tested will fail unpredictably in production.

Launch Readiness GatesStable

A pass/fail checklist for moving from demo to production. Red items block launch.
TL;DR before this long table
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
AreaGateEvidence
IdentityEvery request has user_id, tenant_id, role, session_id, and trace_id.Trace sample + auth integration test.
Data accessRetrieval enforces ACLs before any chunk enters model context.Permission-boundary eval suite.
PromptingSystem prompts are versioned, reviewed, pinned, and eval-tested.PR, changelog, eval report.
SafetyInput, retrieved context, tool arguments, and outputs pass policy checks.Safety evals + red-team cases.
ToolsEach tool has owner, schema, risk tier, permission policy, idempotency, audit log.Tool registry export.
EvalsGolden dataset, regression suite, adversarial suite, and cost tests pass thresholds.CI artifact with baselines.
ObservabilityLogs include model, prompt, retrieval, tool calls, latency, errors, cost, feedback.Dashboard screenshots + trace IDs.
RollbackCan roll back prompt, model, retrieval config, tool version, and UI independently.Rollback drill record.
SupportHuman escalation path, queue owner, SLA, and support macros are live.Test handoff transcript.
CostPer-user, per-session, per-tenant, and global budgets are enforced.Budget tests + alert policy.

Real Config Examples

Production behavior should be configured, reviewed, and released like code. These examples show the minimum shape of the control files.
config/models.yaml
default_model: claude-sonnet-4-20260501
fallback_model: gpt-4.1
budgets:
 max_input_tokens: 32000
 max_cost_usd_per_run: 0.10
routes:
 classify: claude-haiku
 rag_answer: claude-sonnet
 complex_plan: claude-opus
fallbacks:
 on_timeout: use_fallback_model
 on_rate_limit: queue_or_degrade
config/tools.yaml
tools:
 create_refund:
  owner: support_ops
  risk_tier: high
  requires_approval: true
  idempotency_key: required
  allowed_roles: [support_agent]
  audit: true
 search_docs:
  owner: knowledge_platform
  risk_tier: low
  requires_approval: false
  acl_filter: required
config/retrieval.yaml
indexes:
 help_center:
  embedding_model: text-embedding-3-large
  chunk_size: 900
  overlap: 120
  reranker: bge-reranker-large
  top_k: 12
  citation_required: true
  acl_field: allowed_principals
  freshness_days: 90
evals/release.yaml
required_suites:
 - grounded_answer_quality
 - citation_accuracy
 - prompt_injection
 - tool_selection
 - permission_boundary
thresholds:
 grounded_quality: ">= 0.88"
 citation_accuracy: ">= 0.95"
 unsafe_action_rate: "== 0"
 p95_cost_usd: "<= 0.08"

Production Data Schemas

The minimum data contracts required for debugging, auditability, evals, rollback, and typed validation. Implement these contracts as versioned JSON Schema contracts wherever services touch user input, tool calls, retrieval metadata, structured model output, or persisted state.
TL;DR before this long table
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
ObjectRequired fieldsWhy it matters
conversationconversation_id, tenant_id, user_id, channel, created_at, retention_policy, statusSession continuity, deletion, support lookup.
messagemessage_id, conversation_id, role, content_hash, redacted_content, created_atTranscript reconstruction without leaking raw sensitive data.
agent_runrun_id, trace_id, prompt_version, model_version, route, latency_ms, cost_usd, outcomeObservability, eval replay, incident forensics.
retrieved_chunkchunk_id, source_id, doc_version, score, acl_decision, citation_url, freshness_atGrounding, citation accuracy, permission proof.
tool_calltool_call_id, tool_name, schema_version, args_hash, risk_tier, approval_id, idempotency_key, result_statusAction audit, replay prevention, rollback.
structured_output_schemaschema_name, schema_version, json_schema_hash, owner, backwards_compatiblePrevents brittle JSON parsing, catches schema drift, and makes LLM outputs testable.
safety_eventevent_id, policy_name, severity, blocked, detector_version, evidence_hashGovernance, red-team tracking, compliance.
feedback_eventfeedback_id, user_id, run_id, rating, reason_code, free_text_redactedContinuous improvement and eval case mining.
release_artifactrelease_id, git_sha, prompt_version, model_pin, retrieval_config, eval_report_urlReproducibility and rollback.

Ownership / RACI

Every production component needs an accountable owner. AI incidents often happen at the seams between product, platform, data, security, and support.
TL;DR before this long table
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
AreaResponsibleAccountableConsulted / informed
Product behaviorAI product managerBusiness ownerSupport, legal, security
Prompt & context designApplied AI engineerAI product leadDomain experts, eval owner
Model gatewayPlatform engineeringAI platform leadFinance, security
Retrieval qualitySearch/RAG engineerData platform leadKnowledge owners, support
Tool permissionsApplication ownerSecurity ownerProduct, legal, compliance
EvalsEval engineerQuality ownerProduct, support, domain SMEs
Observability & on-callSRE / platformEngineering managerProduct, support
Human escalationSupport operationsSupport leadProduct, engineering
GovernanceRisk / complianceExecutive sponsorLegal, security, product

Environment Strategy

Production AI behavior depends on prompts, models, tools, retrieval indexes, policies, and data. Environments must isolate all of them.
EnvironmentPurposeAllowed data/toolsPromotion requirement
Local devFast iteration, unit tests, prompt experiments.Synthetic data, mocked tools, local fixtures.Lint + unit tests before PR.
Shared devIntegration testing across services.Seed data, sandbox model keys, mock write tools.Integration tests and schema validation.
StagingProduction-like behavior without real customer impact.Scrubbed data, staging indexes, sandbox external systems.Full eval run, load test, rollback drill.
Canary prodLimited real traffic exposure.Real data with full policies and audit logging.Live gates pass for quality, latency, safety, cost.
Full prodGeneral availability.Real data, approved tools, pinned release artifacts.Ongoing monitoring, incident response, periodic eval refresh.
Environment rule: never use production secrets, raw PII, or customer write tools in local/dev. Staging must be close enough to production to catch behavioral failures, but isolated enough that accidental actions cannot affect customers.

Failure-Mode Playbook

The production runbook for common agent and chatbot failures. Each failure needs a detection signal, mitigation, owner, and follow-up eval.
TL;DR before this long table
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
Failure modeDetection signalImmediate mitigationPermanent fix
Hallucinated answerThumbs-down, support correction, contradiction eval failure.Lower confidence threshold; force retrieval/citation or no-answer.Add eval case, improve prompt, retrieval, or source coverage.
Stale retrievalFreshness metric breach; cited doc older than allowed policy.Disable affected index or display freshness warning.Fix ingestion schedule, source connector, or freshness filters.
Permission leakACL eval failure, cross-tenant chunk, audit alert.Kill retrieval feature; invalidate cached context.Patch ACL filter, add boundary tests, review index permissions.
Prompt injectionSafety detector, tool misuse attempt, instruction conflict.Block response/tool call; escalate suspicious session.Harden context isolation and injection eval suite.
Wrong tool callTool-selection eval fail, user complaint, action reversal.Require human approval for that tool; lower autonomy level.Improve schemas, examples, routing, and tool-choice evals.
Duplicate actionRepeated idempotency key, duplicate external transaction.Pause write tool; reconcile affected records.Enforce idempotency, transaction locks, compensation handlers.
Model outageTimeout/rate-limit spike, provider status alert.Fallback model or graceful degraded mode.Multi-provider routing, queueing, and budget-aware retries.
Cost spikeP95 cost per run or tenant budget breach.Throttle, shorten context, route to cheaper model.Add cost tests, semantic cache, retrieval pruning.
Latency spikeP95/P99 SLO breach, streaming start delay.Disable expensive rerank/tools; use faster model route.Parallelize retrieval/tools, cache, optimize prompts.
Memory contaminationUser says memory is wrong; eval finds irrelevant personal context.Disable memory read/write for affected user or tenant.Add memory write policy, expiry, user-visible correction flow.
Citation mismatchCitation audit finds answer unsupported by cited chunks.Require answer-span verification or suppress unsupported claims.Add citation accuracy eval and stricter answer grounding.

AI Risk Register

⚠️Risks that need explicit ownershipoperational register
TL;DR before this long table
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
RiskLikelihoodImpactDetection signalMitigationOwner
Prompt injectionHighHighInstruction-conflict traces, unsafe tool attempts.Context isolation, tool allowlists, injection evals, confirmation gates.Security + AI platform
Data leakageMediumCriticalCross-tenant retrieval, DLP alert, user report.Tenant-scoped indexes, ACL filters, redaction, audit logs.Security + data owner
Retrieval poisoningMediumHighUnexpected source ranking, new corpus anomaly.Source trust scoring, ingestion approvals, freshness and provenance metadata.Data platform
Model regressionMediumHighEval pass-rate drop after model change.Model pinning, canaries, rollback, release artifact tracking.AI platform
Cost runawayHighMediumTenant budget breach, token spike, loop depth spike.Budgets, loop limits, caching, summarization, cheaper routing.Platform + finance owner
Hallucinated actionMediumCriticalWrong tool call, reversal request, failed postcondition.Action schemas, precondition checks, HITL for high-impact tools.Product + operations
Silent eval driftMediumHighProduction complaints not represented in eval suite.Mine feedback into evals, recurring dataset review, label governance.Evaluation owner
Vendor outageMediumHighTimeouts, rate limits, provider status alert.Fallback model, queueing, degraded mode, retry budget.SRE + platform

SLO / SLA Examples

🎚️Make production readiness measurableexample thresholds
TL;DR before this long table
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
MetricPrototypeInternal productionCustomer-facing productionNotes
p95 first-token latency< 5s< 2.5s< 1.5sStreaming UX should start early even when full task takes longer.
p95 full-response latency< 30s< 12s< 8sSeparate simple Q&A from tool-heavy workflows.
Tool-call success rate> 90%> 97%> 99%Measure schema validity, auth success, and postcondition success.
Grounded answer rate> 80%> 92%> 97%For RAG products, unsupported claims should block launch.
Citation accuracy> 85%> 95%> 98%Citation must support the specific answer span, not just the topic.
Unsafe action block rateManual> 99%> 99.5%Track both true positives and false positives.
Cost per successful taskObservedBudgeted by use caseBudgeted by tenant/SKUUse unit economics, not only token totals.
Eval pass threshold> 75%> 90%> 95%High-risk domains require stricter policy and factuality gates.

Eval Dataset Design

🧪What your eval suite should containrelease-grade coverage
TL;DR before this long table
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
Dataset slicePurposeSourceMinimum fields
Golden Q&AVerify expected answers for common tasks.SME-written examples, support history, product docs.Question, expected answer, allowed sources, grading rubric.
Adversarial promptsTest prompt injection, jailbreaks, and unsafe asks.Security team, red-team sessions, production incidents.Prompt, threat category, expected refusal or safe behavior.
Tool-use scenariosValidate schema selection, parameters, and postconditions.Workflow logs, synthetic task plans, API contracts.User goal, expected tool sequence, fixture data, success assertion.
Retrieval-grounded tasksTest whether the system finds and cites the right evidence.Knowledge base, PDFs, tickets, policies.Query, gold chunks, source version, expected answer span.
Multi-turn memory testsVerify personalization without contamination.Scripted conversations, user-consented memory fixtures.Conversation, memory write expectation, memory read expectation.
Tenant-boundary testsPrevent cross-customer data exposure.Permission fixtures, synthetic tenants.User/role, allowed docs, forbidden docs, expected denial.
Regression casesPrevent known failures from reappearing.Thumbs-down feedback, incidents, QA bugs.Original trace, fixed expected behavior, release introduced/fixed.

Privacy & Data Governance Checklist

🔐Controls before sensitive data enters the systemprivacy gate
PII and secrets handling
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Detect, redact, tokenize, or block sensitive values before prompts, traces, logs, memory writes, and eval artifacts store them.
Retention and deletion
Auditreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Define retention windows for prompts, outputs, traces, embeddings, memory, uploaded files, and audit logs. Support right-to-delete workflows where required.
Tenant-scoped retrieval
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Do not rely on post-generation filtering. Apply ACLs at retrieval time, cache time, and memory-read time.
Training-data exclusion
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Document whether prompts, outputs, feedback, and uploaded files may be used for training, fine-tuning, evals, or product analytics.
Consent and transparency
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Tell users when memory, logging, human review, or external tools are active. Provide correction and opt-out paths for stored memory.
Regulated-domain handling
Auditreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Map controls to relevant obligations such as auditability, access review, least privilege, encryption, retention, and human approval for high-impact actions.

Incident Response Templates

🚨Concrete response patternscopy into runbooks
IncidentContainmentInvestigationPermanent fixFollow-up eval
Agent executed wrong tool actionDisable or require approval for the tool; freeze affected workflow.Review trace, prompt, retrieved context, tool schema, and auth scope.Add preconditions, idempotency, approval threshold, and rollback handler.Replay the trace plus variants in tool-use regression suite.
Retrieval served stale or wrong dataDisable affected index or display freshness warning.Compare source version, chunk metadata, retrieval ranking, and index build time.Rebuild index, add freshness filters, improve provenance and source prioritization.Add stale-source and citation-support test cases.
Cross-tenant data exposureShut off retrieval/memory for affected tenant; revoke cached context.Trace ACL path across ingestion, index, retrieval, cache, and prompt assembly.Patch ACL enforcement, isolate indexes if needed, add boundary tests.Run tenant-boundary evals on every release.
Prompt injection bypassed policyBlock the session/tool call; preserve trace for security review.Identify injected source, instruction hierarchy failure, and missing detector.Strengthen context isolation, add detector, reduce tool permissions.Add exact injection and generalized variants to red-team suite.
Model upgrade caused quality dropRollback model route; pin previous model version.Compare eval slices, traces, cost, latency, and policy failures by model.Add model canary, release artifact, and slice-level approval gates.Run model-regression suite before future promotions.
Part VI

Appendix

Shared reference material, glossary, and document version history.

GlossaryStable

📚Common terms used throughout this playbook87 terms
Use this glossary to align product, engineering, security, data, and operations teams on the vocabulary used in agentic AI production systems.
TL;DR before this long table
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
TermMeaning
A2A ProtocolAgent-to-agent communication pattern for coordinating agents across systems, vendors, or execution boundaries.
A2UIAgent-to-UI pattern for generating or controlling user interface components from agent state and events.
ACL-Aware RetrievalRetrieval that filters documents, chunks, and citations according to user, tenant, role, group, or document-level permissions before content reaches the model.
AgentA system that can interpret a goal, reason over context, choose actions, call tools, observe results, and continue until a stop condition or handoff.
Agent FrameworkThe abstraction layer for building agents: model calls, prompt templates, tool binding, structured outputs, message formatting, retrieval helpers, and reusable agent patterns.
Agent HarnessThe outer session or interaction shell that starts an agent run, loads project context, manages the workspace or channel, and returns output to a user or calling system.
Agent RuntimeThe execution engine that manages state over time: checkpoints, retries, graph transitions, fan-out/fan-in, human interrupts, durable execution, and long-running workflow control.
Agentic WorkflowA multi-step workflow where an AI system performs planning, tool use, retrieval, validation, and iteration rather than producing a single response.
AG-UIAn agent-to-user-interface event protocol pattern that lets agents stream state, actions, intermediate outputs, and UI updates to applications.
Approval GateA required human, policy, or automated approval step before a high-risk action, irreversible side effect, or release promotion can continue.
ArtifactA durable output produced by an AI system, such as a report, code diff, plan, summary, data file, chart, or generated document.
Audit LogAn immutable or tamper-evident record of user actions, model calls, tool calls, approvals, policy decisions, and system events used for governance and incident review.
Autonomy LadderA progression from passive suggestions to tool execution with approval, supervised automation, and finally bounded autonomous operation.
Build-vs-BuyA decision framework for choosing whether to adopt an existing managed/tooling layer or build a custom internal component.
Canary ReleaseA controlled rollout where a new model, prompt, tool, or workflow is exposed to a small slice of traffic before broader promotion.
Capability BoundaryThe line where an agent gains access to an external action, data source, resource, or workflow; this boundary should be typed, permissioned, logged, and policy-controlled.
Capability DiscoveryA mechanism by which a host or agent learns what tools, resources, prompts, and schemas are available from an interface such as MCP.
CheckpointerA persistence mechanism that saves agent state at key steps so conversations or workflows can resume, branch, audit, or roll back.
ChunkA segment of a source document prepared for retrieval, usually with metadata such as source, section, tenant, timestamp, and permissions.
Context + Capability InterfaceThe boundary where agents access external capabilities and context through typed tools, resources, prompts, retrieval connectors, OpenAPI schemas, function tools, or MCP servers.
Context EngineeringThe design of what information enters the model context, including instructions, retrieved evidence, memory, tool results, policy, and conversation history.
Context WindowThe maximum amount of input and output text a model can consider in one request, usually measured in tokens.
CopilotKitReact-first framework for embedding AI copilots and agentic workflows inside production applications.
Cost RouterA routing layer that selects cheaper or more capable models depending on task difficulty, latency needs, confidence, and budget constraints.
Cross-Cutting PlaneA concern that spans multiple stack layers rather than living in one layer, such as observability, evaluation, security, governance, privacy, cost, and policy.
DAG / WorkflowA directed acyclic graph that defines deterministic task dependencies; useful when an agentic process should be constrained into known steps.
Data LineageThe traceable path of data from source systems through ingestion, transformation, retrieval, prompts, outputs, and stored artifacts.
DatabricksEnterprise lakehouse and AI platform for governed analytical data, Delta Lake/Unity Catalog access control, feature and ML pipelines, vector search, MLflow evaluation, and model or agent deployment. In this playbook it is a data + governance + AI platform that agents can call into, not a replacement for an orchestration framework.
Durable ExecutionExecution that persists workflow state so a run can survive process crashes, restarts, retries, deployments, or long human approval waits.
EmbeddingA numerical vector representation of text, image, or other content used for semantic search, clustering, deduplication, or similarity matching.
Eval HarnessThe system that runs test cases against prompts, models, retrieval pipelines, tools, and agent trajectories, then records pass/fail and quality metrics.
Execution SubstrateThe actual environment or external system where side effects occur: containers, sandboxes, browsers, Python kernels, filesystems, databases, queues, storage, or SaaS APIs.
Fan-out / Fan-inA runtime pattern where work is split across parallel branches or agents, then joined back into a single downstream step or synthesis phase.
Freshness CheckA validation step that verifies whether retrieved content, source claims, model capabilities, or vendor details are current enough for the decision being made.
Generative UIUmbrella pattern where agents produce interface events, component descriptions, or validated UI schemas that client applications render safely.
Golden DatasetA curated set of test inputs with expected answers, source evidence, rubrics, or tool-call expectations used as a release gate.
GraphRAGA retrieval pattern that combines graph relationships with semantic retrieval so answers can use entity links, dependencies, and multi-hop context.
GroundednessThe degree to which an answer is supported by retrieved or cited source evidence rather than unsupported model inference.
GuardrailA control that constrains model behavior, tool use, outputs, inputs, or policy compliance through rules, classifiers, validators, or human review.
HITLHuman-in-the-loop. A design pattern where humans review, approve, correct, or take over high-risk, low-confidence, or policy-sensitive steps.
IdempotencyA property of an operation where repeated execution has the same effect as one execution, which is critical for safe retries and tool calls.
Inner Behavioral LoopThe repeated decision pattern inside an agent run, such as Think → Act → Observe, ReAct, plan-and-execute, reflection, or evaluator-optimizer loops.
Instruction HierarchyThe ordering of authority among system instructions, developer instructions, user requests, retrieved content, memory, and tool outputs.
InterruptA runtime primitive that pauses execution, serializes state, and waits for a human approval, correction, external event, or policy decision before continuing.
MCPModel Context Protocol; a standard interface pattern for connecting agents or model applications to tools, data sources, prompts, resources, and external systems.
MCP AppsMCP-native app-surface pattern that packages tools, resources, and workflows into user-facing experiences.
MCP-UIPattern for returning interactive UI surfaces through MCP tool or resource contexts.
MemoryStored user, task, or system information that can be recalled across turns or sessions, often separated into short-term, long-term, episodic, or semantic memory.
Model FallbackA model-specific fallback path that routes to another model when the primary model fails, times out, exceeds budget, violates policy, or scores below confidence thresholds.
Model Gateway / AI GatewayA model-access control layer between applications, agents, and model providers. It centralizes provider abstraction, routing, retries, fallbacks, virtual keys, budgets, spend tracking, logging, caching, and policy hooks. Examples include LiteLLM, Portkey, OpenRouter, Kong AI Gateway, and Cloudflare AI Gateway.
LiteLLMAn open-source AI gateway and Python SDK that provides a unified OpenAI-compatible interface across many model providers. In production, it is commonly used as a centralized proxy for model routing, virtual keys, budgets, spend tracking, fallbacks, caching, and logging.
Model RegressionA quality, safety, cost, or latency drop introduced by changing the model, prompt, retrieval pipeline, tool schema, or orchestration logic.
Model RouterA routing layer that chooses a model based on task type, cost, latency, context size, modality, risk, region, or required capability.
Model RoutingThe process of selecting which model or provider should handle a request based on task type, cost, latency, availability, policy, data boundary, or fallback rules.
Multi-Agent OrchestrationCoordination of multiple agents with specialized roles, shared state, message passing, task delegation, and conflict resolution.
Observability TraceA structured record of a model or agent run, including prompts, outputs, retrieval hits, tool calls, latency, costs, errors, and policy events.
Open-JSON-UIDeclarative JSON contract for generated UI components, layouts, bindings, and actions.
OPAOpen Policy Agent; a policy engine commonly used for authorization, entitlements, and governance decisions.
OpenTelemetry / OTELA vendor-neutral telemetry standard for traces, metrics, and logs that helps connect agent traces with infrastructure and application observability.
PIIPersonally identifiable information; data that can identify, contact, locate, or distinguish a person, directly or in combination with other data.
Policy-as-CodeEncoding access, safety, privacy, approval, and governance rules in versioned, testable policy logic rather than only in prose or manual process.
PostconditionA condition that must be true after a tool call or workflow step, such as record created, email sent, balance unchanged, or approval logged.
Product / Control PlaneThe layer above the agent harness that owns product workflow, user/org permissions, workflow catalog, approval queues, quotas, audit posture, launch gates, and governance controls.
Prompt InjectionAn attack where malicious or untrusted content tries to override instructions, reveal secrets, misuse tools, or alter system behavior.
Prompt Resource / Prompt TemplateA reusable prompt, instruction block, or template exposed by a framework, host, or MCP server for consistent agent behavior.
PromptFooAn evaluation and red-team testing tool used to compare prompts, models, outputs, and policy behaviors across test cases.
RAGRetrieval-augmented generation. A pattern where the system retrieves external knowledge and supplies it to the model to improve accuracy and provenance.
RBACRole-based access control. A permission model where capabilities are assigned to roles and users inherit the permissions of their roles.
Red-Team SuiteA set of adversarial tests designed to probe unsafe behavior, security failures, jailbreaks, data leaks, and policy bypasses.
Release ArtifactA versioned record of the model, prompt, tools, policies, retrieval index, eval results, and deployment metadata used in a release.
ResourceIn MCP and related systems, a context object or data surface exposed for reading by a host or model application, distinct from a callable tool.
Retrieval PoisoningA failure or attack where misleading, malicious, stale, or unauthorized content enters the retrieval corpus and influences model outputs.
SandboxAn isolated execution environment for risky work such as code execution, shell commands, browser automation, file mutation, or data queries, with limits on filesystem, network, secrets, and resources.
Semantic SearchSearch based on meaning or similarity rather than exact keyword matching, usually powered by embeddings and vector indexes.
Side EffectA change caused outside the model context, such as writing a file, sending an email, updating a ticket, running code, querying a database, charging a card, or calling an external API.
SLO / SLAService-level objective / agreement. An SLO is an internal target; an SLA is a commitment that may carry external or contractual consequences.
SnowflakeEnterprise data cloud and AI platform for governed warehouse data, SQL analytics, Cortex Analyst text-to-SQL, Cortex Search retrieval, Cortex Agents, UDF/stored-procedure tools, and Streamlit-style internal data apps. In this playbook it is a business data + governance + AI platform that agents can use as a trusted substrate.
Source Maintenance DisciplineThe practice of separating stable architecture guidance from volatile market claims and requiring source URLs, owners, and last-checked dates for claims likely to drift.
Tenant BoundaryThe isolation line between customers, teams, users, or environments that prevents data, memory, permissions, and traces from crossing improperly.
Tenant-Scoped AccessAccess control that restricts retrieval, memory, tools, traces, and artifacts to the tenant, workspace, environment, or organization authorized for the current user/run.
Think → Act → ObserveThe inner agent loop where the model decides what to do, calls a tool or produces an action, observes the result, and continues until a stop condition.
Tool InterfaceThe typed boundary presented to the agent for calling an action, including name, description, parameters, output schema, permissions, and error behavior.
Tool SchemaThe structured contract for a tool, including name, description, input parameters, output shape, validation rules, and permission requirements.
Trajectory EvalAn evaluation of the full path an agent takes: reasoning steps, retrieval, tool calls, intermediate states, errors, and final answer.
Vector DatabaseA database or search layer optimized for storing embeddings and retrieving nearest-neighbor matches for semantic search.
Virtual KeyA gateway-issued key used to control model access, budgets, rate limits, team/user attribution, and spend tracking without exposing raw provider credentials to every application.
Workflow FallbackA predefined alternative path when a model, tool, retrieval source, or agent step fails or falls below confidence thresholds.

Vendor Risk / Procurement Checklist

📋Checks before adopting a model, agent platform, vector database, or eval toolprocurement
AreaQuestions to answer
Security postureSOC 2 / ISO 27001 status, vulnerability disclosure process, SSO / SCIM, audit logs, admin controls.
Data policyRetention period, training-on-customer-data policy, regional residency, deletion SLA, export path.
Operational reliabilitySLA, rate limits, incident history, status page, support tier, backup and disaster recovery plan.
Lock-in / exitData export, prompt/tool portability, model fallback, schema ownership, migration cost.
Commercial riskPricing volatility, minimum commitments, overage terms, enterprise contract protections.
Open-source riskLicense compatibility, maintainer activity, dependency health, SBOM, container provenance.

Version History

🗂️Document Version Historycurrent · v3.34
v3.34GraphRAG depth pass — 10 additions across graph DB and GraphRAG coverage. (1) GraphRAG vs RAG decision table (retrieval unit, cost, latency, explainability, schema requirement, when not to use). (2) Graph knowledge construction pipeline: 6-stage extract→relation→resolve→load→vector-index→update with failure modes and tooling. (3) GraphRAG chunking considerations: entity boundary preservation, co-reference window, dual chunking strategies, source provenance. (4) Three named hybrid retrieval patterns: entity-anchored, community summarization, and path-constrained — with cost tradeoffs. (5) Graph schema design card in Data Substrate: nodes vs relationships vs text placement, schema-from-questions rule. (6) Incremental graph update table: new doc, updated doc, deleted doc, new alias, community change, schema change. (7) GraphRAG cost model section: index-build vs query-time cost comparison across standard RAG, traversal approach, community summarization. (8) Neptune Analytics clarification: Neptune Database vs Neptune Analytics as two separate services. (9) Apache AGE maturity caveats: Cypher coverage gaps, no native vector search, community/release cadence, when to choose vs Neo4j. (10) Four new GraphRAG anti-pattern cards: schema for completeness not retrieval, using GraphRAG for simple Q&A, duplicating document text into nodes, no entity resolution before load. TOC updated with new section anchors.
v3.33Version hygiene and usability pass. Aligned visible version labels across header, TOC, executive summary, footer, metadata, and version history; added modular reading modes; added production checklist blocks for launch, evals, security, and cost; refreshed document-guide language and maintenance metadata.
v3.32TOC ordering pass. In Guide: moved Choose Your Path to top; moved For ML & Data Science Practitioners from System Overview / Foundational AI Product Patterns to Guide section (bottom). In Production Playbook: moved Async Job Queue Architecture immediately after Canonical Production Architecture (matching document order); swapped Pattern Composition Diagram and Pattern Selection Matrix to match document order.
v3.31Layout and consistency pass. Set Neo4j and Amazon Neptune side by side (2-col grid) in the Agentic AI System Map Knowledge & Retrieval layer. Set OpenSearch, pgvector, and pgvectorscale on one row (3-col grid) in the Projects Knowledge & Retrieval section. Set Neo4j, Amazon Neptune, and Apache AGE on one row (3-col grid) in the Projects Graph Traversal section. Moved v3.29 and v3.30 version entries into the version history card and matched their formatting. Trimmed version history to the last 5 entries. Fixed stale v3.29 header badge. Moved For ML & Data Science Practitioners from the Guide TOC group to System Overview.
v3.30Accuracy and quality pass. Corrected AG-UI attribution from "Anthropic / community" to "CopilotKit / community". Fixed 6 glossary alphabetization errors in the appendix glossary. Added a Current freshness badge to the Async Job Queue Architecture section heading.
Agentic AI System · May 202686 projects · 47 production sections · v3.34
